title: EXPOSÉ II EXISTENCE OF GLOBAL RESOLUTIONS

pages.title: EXPOSÉ II EXISTENCE OF GLOBAL RESOLUTIONS

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ II
EXISTENCE OF GLOBAL RESOLUTIONSby L. Illusie
1. General globalization criteria

In this section, we are given a site \(\underline S\), a flat abelian \(\underline S\)-category \(\underline C\) (I 1.1), and a strictly full additive sub-\(\underline S\)-category \(\underline C_0\) (I 1.1). We assume \(\underline C_0\) is locally quasi-liftable in \(\underline C\) (I 1.2) and locally quasi-stable under kernels of epimorphisms (I 1.2).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.1.

Let \(S\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), and suppose that:

(i) \(\underline C_{0S}\) is quasi-liftable in \(\underline C_S\) (I 1.2) (\(\underline C_S\) being regarded as a fibered category over a punctual site);

(ii) every object of \(\underline C_S\) which is of \(\underline C_0\)-finite type (I 1.2) (resp. of \(\underline C_0\)-finite presentation (I 2.8)) is of \(\underline C_{0S}\)-finite type.

Then:

a) \(\underline C_{0S}\) is quasi-stable under kernels of epimorphisms (I 1.2) (\(\underline C_{0S}\) being regarded as a fibered category over a punctual site).

b) Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline C_S)\) and \(n\in\mathbb Z\). If \(E\) is \(n\)-\(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent (I 2.3), then \(E\) is \(n\)-\(\underline C_{0S}\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. \((n+1)\)-\(\underline C_{0S}\)-pseudo-coherent). In other words, we have

\[D^-(\underline C_S)_{n-\underline C_0\text{-coh}} \subset D^-(\underline C_S)_{n-\underline C_{0S}\text{-coh}}\]

(resp.

\[D^-(\underline C_S)_{n-\underline C_0\text{-coh}} \subset D^-(\underline C_S)_{(n+1)-\underline C_{0S}\text{-coh}} \subset D^-(\underline C_S)_{(n+1)-\underline C_0\text{-coh}},\]

inclusions of strictly full subcategories of \(D(\underline C_S)\), the right-hand inclusion being moreover trivial).

c) For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline C_S)\) to be pseudo-coherent relative to \(\underline C_0\) (I 2.3), it is necessary and sufficient that it be so relative to \(\underline C_{0S}\); in other words, we have

\[D^-(\underline C_S)_{\underline C_0\text{-coh}} =D^-(\underline C_S)_{\underline C_{0S}\text{-coh}}.\]

Moreover, the canonical functor

\[K^-(\underline C_{0S})/K^{-,\varnothing}(\underline C_{0S})\longrightarrow D(\underline C_S),\]

where \(K^{-,\varnothing}(\underline C_{0S})\) denotes the thick subcategory of \(K^-(\underline C_{0S})\) formed by the acyclic complexes, is fully faithful and has essential image \(D^-(\underline C_S)_{\underline C_0\text{-coh}}\). If \(\underline C_{0S}\) is liftable in \(\underline C_S\) (I 1.2) (i.e. if the objects of \(\underline C_{0S}\) are projective), the category \(K^{-,\varnothing}(\underline C_{0S})\) is reduced to the zero objects, and consequently the functor

\[K^-(\underline C_{0S})\longrightarrow D(\underline C_S)\]

is fully faithful (and has essential image \(D^-(\underline C_S)_{\underline C_0\text{-coh}}\)).
Proof. a) Let
\[0\longrightarrow L\longrightarrow L^0\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow L^m\longrightarrow0\]

be an exact sequence of \(\underline C_S\), with \(L^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0S}\) for every \(i\); the point is to show that \(L\) is of \(\underline C_{0S}\)-finite type. Now, by (I 2.2), \(L\) is \(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent, hence (I 2.9) of \(\underline C_0\)-finite presentation, and the assertion follows from (ii).

b) Let us show, by descending induction on \(i\), that \(E\) is \(i\)-\(\underline C_{0S}\)-pseudo-coherent for \(i\geq n\) (resp. \(i\geq n+1\)). We may already assume \(E\) strictly \((n+1)\)-\(\underline C_{0S}\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. \((n+2)\)-\(\underline C_{0S}\)-pseudo-coherent) (I 2.1), and the point is to prove that \(E\) is \(n\)-\(\underline C_{0S}\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. \((n+1)\)-\(\underline C_{0S}\)-pseudo-coherent). We thus have an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow E'\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow E''\longrightarrow0,\]

where \(E'\) is strictly \(\underline C_{0S}\)-perfect (I 2.1) and \(E''^i=0\) for \(i>n\) (resp. \(i>n+1\)). Replacing \(E\) by \(E''\) if necessary, we may therefore assume (I 2.6) that \(E^i=0\) for \(i>n\) (resp. \(i>n+1\)). Then, by (I 2.10 a)), \(H^n(E)\) (resp. \(H^{n+1}(E)\)) is of \(\underline C_0\)-finite type (resp. of \(\underline C_0\)-finite presentation). Hence, by (ii), \(H^n(E)\) (resp. \(H^{n+1}(E)\)) is of \(\underline C_{0S}\)-finite type. Applying (I 2.10 b)) again, we deduce that \(E\) is \(n\)-\(\underline C_{0S}\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. \((n+1)\)-\(\underline C_{0S}\)-pseudo-coherent), qed. (N.B. hypothesis (i) and assertion a) have been used implicitly in the references to (I 2.6 and 2.10)).
c) The first assertion follows from b), the second from (I 2.7); the last is obvious.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2.

Suppose \(\underline C_0\) is locally liftable in \(\underline C\) (I 1.2) and locally stable under kernels of epimorphisms (I 1.2), and let us make the hypotheses (1.1 (i) and (ii) resp.). Denote by \(\underline C_{1S}\) the strictly full subcategory of \(\underline C_S\) formed by the objects which are locally in \(\underline C_0\). Then:

a) \(\underline C_{1S}\) is stable under kernels of epimorphisms (I 1.2) and quasi-liftable in \(\underline C_S\) (I 1.2) (as a fibered category over a punctual site).

b) Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C_S)\) and \([m,n]\) be an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). For \(E\) to be of \(\underline C_0\)-perfect amplitude contained in \([m,n]\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(E\) be isomorphic, in \(D(\underline C_S)\), to a complex \(F\) such that \(F^i=0\) for \(i\notin[m,n]\), \(F^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0S}\) for \(i>m\), and \(F^m\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{1S}\).

c) The canonical functor

\[K^b(\underline C_{1S})/K^{b,\varnothing}(\underline C_{1S})\longrightarrow D(\underline C_S)\]

is fully faithful and has as essential image the full subcategory of \(D(\underline C_S)\) formed by the complexes of finite \(\underline C_0\)-perfect amplitude.

d) If \(\underline C_{1S}\) is not only quasi-liftable but liftable in \(\underline C_S\), one may replace in b) the expression "\(E\) be isomorphic to \(F\) in \(D(\underline C_S)\)" by "there exists a quasi-isomorphism \(F\longrightarrow E\)", and in c) the category \(K^{b,\varnothing}(\underline C_{1S})\) is reduced to the zero objects.

Proof. a) It is clear that \(\underline C_{1S}\) is stable under kernels of epimorphisms. On the other hand, the objects of \(\underline C_{1S}\) are \(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent, hence \(\underline C_{0S}\)-pseudo-coherent by (1.1 c)); the second assertion therefore follows from (I 2.14) (where we take for \(S\) the punctual site).

b) It suffices to prove the necessity. We already know, thanks to (1.1 c)), that \(E\) is \(\underline C_{0S}\)-pseudo-coherent. Since \(E\) is acyclic in degree \(>n\), we may therefore assume (I 2.10), replacing \(E\) if necessary by an isomorphic complex in \(D^-(\underline C_S)\), that \(E\) is strictly \((m+1)\)-\(\underline C_{0S}\)-pseudo-coherent and that \(E^i=0\) for \(i>n\). Since \(E\) is acyclic in degree \(<m\), we may moreover assume, replacing \(E\) if necessary by its truncation
\[0\longrightarrow E^m/B^m\longrightarrow E^{m+1}\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow E^n\longrightarrow0,\]
that \(E^i=0\) for \(i<m\). Then, by (I 4.16), \(E^m\) is locally an object of \(\underline C_0\), hence an object of \(\underline C_{1S}\), which proves the assertion.
c) Taking (1.2 a)) into account, the assertion follows from (1.2 b)) and (I 4.12.2).
d) The fact that \(K^{b,\varnothing}(\underline C_{1S})\) is reduced to the zero objects is obvious. The other assertion follows from (I 4.6) (applied over the punctual site).

pages.title: 2. Application to certain categories of Modules

blocks.html: 2. Application to certain categories of Modules
2.0. Notations

Let \((\underline S,A)\) be a ringed topos. For \(U\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), we denote by \(\operatorname{Mod}(U)\) the category of left \(A_U\)-Modules, and by \(\operatorname{Lib}(U)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Loclib}(U)\)) the full subcategory of \(\operatorname{Mod}(U)\) formed by the \(A_U\)-Modules which are free (resp. locally direct factors of free ones) of finite type.
2.1. Lifting lemmas

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.1 (cf. EGA 0I 5.2.3).

Let \((\underline S,A)\) be a ringed topos, and \(S\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\). We assume that \(S\) is quasi-compact, i.e. that every covering family \((S_i\longrightarrow S)\) is dominated by a finite covering family.

a) Let \(F\) be a left \(A_S\)-Module which is an inductive limit, over a filtered category \(\Lambda\), of \(A_S\)-Modules \(F_\lambda\), and let

\[u=\varinjlim u_\lambda:F\longrightarrow G\]

be an epimorphism, where \(G\) is an \(A_S\)-Module of finite type. Then there exists \(\lambda\in\Lambda\) such that \(u_\lambda\) is an epimorphism.

b) Let \(F\) be a left \(A_S\)-Module generated by its sections (i.e. such that there exists an epimorphism \(A_S^{(I)}\longrightarrow F\) for a suitable index set \(I\)). Then, if \(F\) is of finite type, there exists an epimorphism \(A_S^p\longrightarrow F\) for \(p\in\mathbb N\).
The proof of a) is analogous to that of (loc. cit.) and left as an exercise to the reader, and b) follows at once from a).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.2.

Let \((\underline S,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(\underline C\) the fibered \(\underline S\)-category of left \(A\)-Modules, \(\underline C_0\) the fibered subcategory of free \(A\)-Modules of finite type (I 1.3 d)), \(S\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), and \(\underline C'_S\) a strictly full additive subcategory of \(\underline C_S\) containing \(\underline C_{0S}\) and stable under kernels, cokernels and extensions. We assume that

\[H^1(S,F)=0\qquad\text{pour tout }F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C'_S.\]

Then:

a) Every exact sequence of \(\underline C'_S\),

\[0\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow0,\]

such that \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(G,E)\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C'_S\) and such that \(G\) is locally a direct factor of a free Module of finite type, is split. The category \(\underline C_{0S}\) is liftable in \(\underline C'_S\) (I 1.2).

b) Denote by \(\operatorname{Mod}(A(S))\) the category of \(A(S)\)-modules. The functor

\[\Gamma_S:\underline C'_S\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(A(S))\]

is exact. Moreover:

(i) If every object \(F\) of \(\underline C'_S\) admits a global finite presentation

\[A_S^p\longrightarrow A_S^q\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow0,\]

the functor \(\Gamma_S:\underline C'_S\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(A(S))\) is fully faithful and has as essential image the full subcategory of \(\operatorname{Mod}(A(S))\) formed by the \(A(S)\)-modules of finite presentation;

(ii) if \(\underline C'_S\) contains the free \(A_S\)-Modules, if every object \(F\) of \(\underline C'_S\) admits a global presentation

\[A_S^{(I)}\longrightarrow A_S^{(J)}\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow0,\]

and if the functor \(\Gamma_S\) commutes with filtered inductive limits(1), the functor \(\Gamma_S:\underline C'_S\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(A(S))\) is an equivalence of categories.
Proof. a) Since \(G\) is locally a direct factor of a free Module of finite type, the spectral sequence
\[E_2^{pq}=H^p\bigl(S,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^q(G,E)\bigr)\Longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^{p+q}(G,E)\]
degenerates (I 1.3.1) and gives an isomorphism
\[H^1\bigl(S,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(G,E)\bigr)\simeq\operatorname{Ext}^1(G,E),\]

whence the first assertion. The second is a special case of it, since, for \(G\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0S}\), i.e. \(G\) free and of finite type, and \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C'_S\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(G,E)\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C'_S\).

b) The first assertion is trivial. Let us now prove, in case (i) or (ii), the full faithfulness of \(\Gamma_S:\underline C'_S\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(A(S))\). Let \(F,G\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C'_S\), and let
\[(*)\qquad A_S^{(I)}\longrightarrow A_S^{(J)}\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow0\]

be a global presentation of \(F\) (with \(I\) and \(J\) finite in case (i)). Applying to \((*)\) the functor \(\Gamma_S\), which is exact on \(\underline C'_S\) and commutes, in case (ii), with filtered inductive limits, we obtain an exact sequence
\[(**)\qquad A(S)^{(I)}\longrightarrow A(S)^{(J)}\longrightarrow\Gamma_S(F)\longrightarrow0.\]

On the other hand, applying to \((*)\) (resp. \((**)\)) the left exact functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A_S}(\cdot,G)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Hom}_{A(S)}(\cdot,\Gamma_S(G))\)), we obtain a commutative diagram in which the rows are exact:

\[\xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&{\operatorname{Hom}_{A_S}(F,G)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\operatorname{Hom}_{A_S}(A_S^{(J)},G)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\operatorname{Hom}_{A_S}(A_S^{(I)},G)}\ar[d]\\ 0\ar[r]&{\operatorname{Hom}_{A(S)}(\Gamma_S(F),\Gamma_S(G))}\ar[r]&{\operatorname{Hom}_{A(S)}(A(S)^{(J)},\Gamma_S(G))}\ar[r]&{\operatorname{Hom}_{A(S)}(A(S)^{(I)},\Gamma_S(G)).} }\]

The two right-hand vertical arrows are visibly isomorphisms; the same therefore holds for the left-hand vertical arrow, which proves the full faithfulness of \(\Gamma_S:\underline C'_S\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(A(S))\). The characterization of the essential image, in case (i) or (ii), is immediate and left to the reader.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.3.

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be abelian categories, and \(\phi:A\longrightarrow B\) an exact functor.

a) For \(M\in\operatorname{ob}A\), the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) for every \(L\in\operatorname{ob}A\), the canonical arrow (induced by \(\phi\))

\[\operatorname{Ext}_A^n(L,M)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}_B^n(\phi(L),\phi(M))\]

is an isomorphism for every \(n\);

(ii) for every \(L\in\operatorname{ob}A\), the canonical arrow

\[\operatorname{Hom}_A(L,M)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_B(\phi(L),\phi(M))\]

is an isomorphism, and for every \(n\geq1\) and every \(x\in\operatorname{Ext}_B^n(\phi(L),\phi(M))\), there exists an epimorphism \(L'\longrightarrow L\) of \(A\) which effaces \(x\).

b) The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) for all \(L,M\in\operatorname{ob}A\) and every \(n\in\mathbb Z\), the canonical arrow

\[\operatorname{Ext}_A^n(L,M)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}_B^n(\phi(L),\phi(M))\]

is an isomorphism;

(ii) the canonical functor (induced by \(\phi\))

\[D^b(A)\longrightarrow D^b(B)\]

is fully faithful and has as essential image the full subcategory of \(D^b(B)\) formed by the complexes \(F\) such that \(H^i(F)\) is in the essential image of \(\phi\) for every \(i\).

Proof. a) For the implication (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii), it suffices to show the following: let \(n\geq1\), \(L\in\operatorname{ob}A\), and \(y\in\operatorname{Ext}_A^n(L,M)\); then there exists an epimorphism \(L'\longrightarrow L\) which effaces \(y\). For this, one may use the Yoneda interpretation ([18] III 3.2): \(y\) is the class of an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow E_{n-1}\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow E_0\longrightarrow L\longrightarrow0\ ;\]
the epimorphism \(E_0\longrightarrow L\) effaces \(y\) ([18] III 3.2.5 and 3.2.8). Let us prove (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i). Let us set, for brevity,
\[\operatorname{Ext}_A^n(\cdot,M)=F^n,\qquad \operatorname{Ext}_B^n(\phi(\cdot),\phi(M))=G^n.\]
The effaceability hypothesis means that we have, for \(n\geq1\), and \(L\in\operatorname{ob}A\),
\[G^n(L)=\varinjlim\operatorname{Coker}\bigl(G^{n-1}(L_0)\longrightarrow G^{n-1}(L_1)\bigr),\]
the inductive limit being taken over the set of exact sequences
\[0\longrightarrow L_1\longrightarrow L_0\longrightarrow L\longrightarrow0.\]
By what we saw above, we likewise have
\[F^n(L)=\varinjlim\operatorname{Coker}\bigl(F^{n-1}(L_0)\longrightarrow F^{n-1}(L_1)\bigr).\]

Since \(\phi=\phi^0:F^0\longrightarrow G^0\) is an isomorphism, it follows, by induction on \(n\), that the morphism \(\phi^n:F^n\longrightarrow G^n\) induced by \(\phi\) is an isomorphism for every \(n\).
b) The equivalence (i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (ii) follows from an immediate dévissage, left to the reader.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 2.1.3.1.

There is of course an equivalence dual to a), which we leave to the reader to state. On the other hand, if \(A\) and \(B\) have enough injectives, conditions (i) and (ii) of b) are equivalent to

(iii) for \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A)\) and \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(A)\), the canonical arrow

\[\mathbf R\operatorname{Hom}(L,M)\longrightarrow\mathbf R\operatorname{Hom}(\phi(L),\phi(M))\]

is an isomorphism, as one sees at once by a spectral sequence argument.
2.2. Noetherian schemes and divisorial schemes
Notations 2.2.1.

Let \(S\) be a scheme. We denote by \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Coh}(S)\)) the (abelian) category of quasi-coherent (resp. coherent) \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules. Let \([m,n]\) be an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). We shall say that an object \(E\) of \(D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent, resp. of perfect amplitude contained in \([m,n]\), resp. perfect) if \(E\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent, resp. of perfect amplitude contained in \([m,n]\), resp. perfect) relative to the fibered category over the Zariski site of \(S\) defined by \(U\longmapsto\operatorname{Loclib}(U)\) ((I 2.3), (I 4.7) and (2.0)). We shall denote by \(D^-(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (resp. \(D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))_{\mathrm{parf}}\)) the full triangulated subcategory of \(D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\) formed by the pseudo-coherent (resp. perfect) complexes.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.2(*).

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme. The canonical functor

\[D^-(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\longrightarrow D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\]

is fully faithful and has essential image \(D^-(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))_{\mathrm{coh}}\).

Proof. We apply (1.1), taking for \(\underline S\) the Zariski site of \(S\), for \(\underline C\) (resp. \(\underline C_0\)) the fibered category \(U\longmapsto\operatorname{Qcoh}(U)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Coh}(U)\)). Since \(\mathcal O_S\) is coherent, the objects of \(\underline C_0\) are pseudo-coherent (I 3.5), so an object of \(D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(U))\) (\(U\) an open of \(S\)) is pseudo-coherent \(au sens (2.2.1)\) if and only if it is so relative to \(\underline C_0\) (I 2.14.1). By virtue of (1.1 c)), it therefore suffices to show that conditions (i) and (ii) (non-resp. case) of (1.1) are satisfied. For (ii), this is trivial, since a quasi-coherent Module which is a quotient of a coherent Module is coherent, and since the notion of coherence is local. For (i), we use (2.1.1 a)) and the fact that every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module is a filtered inductive limit of coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules (EGA I 9.4.9).
Assuming (3.5) and (3.7), which are independent of the present section, one easily deduces from this:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2.2.1.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme (resp. noetherian and of finite dimension). The canonical functor

\[D^b(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\] \[\text{(resp. }D^-(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\text{)}\]

is fully faithful and has essential image \(D^b(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (resp. \(D^-(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\)) (I 2.15).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.3 (cf. EGA II 4.5.2 and 4.5.5).

Let \(S\) be a quasi-compact quasi-separated scheme, and let \((L_i)_{i\in I}\) be a family of invertible sheaves on \(S\). For every \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module \(F\), and every pair \((i,n)\in I\times\mathbb Z\), we denote by \(F_{(i,n)}\) the sub-\(\mathcal O_S\)-Module of \(F\otimes L_i^{\otimes n}\) generated by the sections of \(F\otimes L_i^{\otimes n}\) over \(S\). The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) The opens \(S_f\), for \(f\in\Gamma(S,L_i^{\otimes n})\), \(i\in I\) and \(n\in\mathbb N\), form a basis of the topology of \(S\).

\(i'\) Those of the \(S_f\) which are affine cover \(S\).

(ii) For every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module \(F\) of finite type, there exists a family \((r_i)_{i\in I}\) (resp. \((n_i)_{i\in I}\)) of integers \(\geq0\) (resp. \(>0\)), and an epimorphism

\[\bigoplus_{i\in I}\mathcal O_S^{r_i}\otimes L_i^{\otimes(-n_i)}\longrightarrow F.\]

(iii) For every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\), we have

\[F=\sum_{(i,n)\in I\times\mathbb N}F_{(i,n)}\otimes L_i^{\otimes(-n)}\]

(sum (not direct) of subsheaves of \(F\)).

(iii') Same condition as (iii), but with \(F\) a quasi-coherent Ideal of \(\mathcal O_S\).
Proof. It consists of a simple paraphrase of (loc. cit.); we give it, briefly, for the convenience of the reader.

(i) \(\Rightarrow\) \(i'\): For \(x\in S\), we choose an affine open \(U\) containing \(x\). An open \(S_f\) such that \(x\in S_f\subset U\), with \(f\in\Gamma(S,L_i^{\otimes n})\) (\(n\geq0\)), is necessarily affine, as follows from the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2.3.1. (EGA II 5.5.8).

Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(L\) an invertible \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module, \(f\in\Gamma(X,L)\). The canonical immersion \(j:X_f\longrightarrow X\) is affine.
Proof. This is trivial: the question is local on \(X\), so we may assume \(X\) affine and \(L\simeq\mathcal O_X\), and we win.

\(i'\) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii): Take a finite covering of \(S\) by affine opens \(S_{f_{ij}}\), where \(f_{ij}\in\Gamma(S,L_i^{\otimes m_{ij}})\); set \(S_i=\bigcup_jS_{f_{ij}}\). Replacing the \(f_{ij}\) by suitable powers if necessary, we may assume that the \(m_{ij}\) corresponding to a given index \(i\) are equal to an integer \(m_i\). The sheaf \(F|S_{f_{ij}}\) is generated by a finite number of sections \(h_{ijk}\) over \(S_{f_{ij}}\). There exists (EGA I 9.3.1 and IV 1.7.5) an integer \(q_i\geq0\) such that \(h_{ijk}\otimes f_{ij}^{\otimes q_i}\) extends to a section of \(F\otimes L_i^{\otimes q_im_i}\) over \(S\). In other words, \(F\otimes L_i^{\otimes q_im_i}\) is generated over \(S_i\) by a finite number of its sections over \(S\). Proceeding as in the proof of (EGA II 4.5.5), we deduce that there exists an integer \(n_i\), which we may assume \(>0\), such that, for every \(n\geq n_i\), \(F\otimes L_i^{\otimes n}\) is generated over \(S_i\) by a finite number of its sections over \(S\). In particular, there exists an integer \(r_i\geq0\) and a morphism
\[\mathcal O_S^{r_i}\otimes L_i^{\otimes(-n_i)}\longrightarrow F\]
which induces an epimorphism over \(S_i\). The implication \(i'\) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii) follows at once.

(ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii): Since every quasi-coherent sheaf on \(S\) is an inductive limit of its quasi-coherent subsheaves of finite type (EGA I 9.4.9 and IV 1.7.7), we may restrict ourselves to proving (iii) for \(F\) of finite type. There then exist, for \(i\in I\), morphisms
\[u_i:\mathcal O_S^{r_i}\otimes L_i^{\otimes(-n_i)}\longrightarrow F\]
such that \(\sum_i u_i\) is an epimorphism. Now we have

\[\begin{aligned} \operatorname{Im}(u_i)&=\operatorname{Im}\bigl(u_i\otimes\operatorname{Id}(L_i^{\otimes n_i})\otimes\operatorname{Id}(L_i^{\otimes(-n_i)})\bigr)\\ &=\operatorname{Im}\bigl(u_i\otimes\operatorname{Id}(L_i^{\otimes n_i})\bigr)\otimes L_i^{\otimes(-n_i)}. \end{aligned}\]
But \(\operatorname{Im}(u_i\otimes\operatorname{Id}(L_i^{\otimes n_i}))\) is a subsheaf of \(F_{(i,n_i)}\), whence the assertion.
(iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii'): This is trivial.

(iii') \(\Rightarrow\) (i): Let \(x\in S\), \(U\) an open containing \(x\), \(F\) a quasi-coherent Ideal defining \(S-U\). By (iii'), there exist \((i,n)\in I\times\mathbb N^+\) and a section \(f\) of \(F\otimes L_i^{\otimes n}\) over \(S\) such that \(f(x)\neq0\). We have \(x\in S_f\subset U\), which proves (i).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.2.4.

Let \(S\) be a quasi-compact quasi-separated scheme, and let \((L_i)_{i\in I}\) be a family of invertible sheaves on \(S\). We say that the \(L_i\) form an ample family(*) if the equivalent conditions of (2.2.3) are satisfied. (In particular a family reduced to a single sheaf \(L\) is ample if and only if \(L\) is ample in the usual sense (EGA II 4.5.3)).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.2.5.

We say that a scheme \(S\) is divisorial if \(S\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated and if there exists an ample family (2.2.4) of invertible \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2.6.

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian scheme. Let \(U\) be an open of \(X\) such that the inclusion \(U\longrightarrow X\) is affine (for example \(U\) affine and \(X\) separated). Then \(X-U\) is purely of codimension 0 or 1 (i.e. at every maximal point \(x\) of \(X-U\) we have \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X,x})=0\) or 1), hence purely of codimension 1 if \(U\) contains the maximal points of \(X\).
Proof. Set \(Y=X-U\). Let \(x\) be a maximal point of \(Y\); let us make the base change \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X,x})\longrightarrow X\): we obtain a cartesian diagram:
\[\xymatrix{ U'\ar[r]\ar[d]&X'&Y'\ar[l]\ar[d]\\ U\ar[r]&X&Y\ar[l] }\]

where \(Y'=X'-U'\). Since \(x\) is a maximal point of \(Y\), \(Y'\) is nothing other than the closed point of \(X'\). On the other hand, since \(X'\) is affine, \(U'\) is an affine open of \(X'\). Let us show that the dimension \(n\) of \(X'\) is equal to 0 or 1. Indeed, we know (SGA 2 V 5.7) that \(H_{Y'}^n(X',\mathcal O_{X'})\neq0\). But we have, if \(n\geq2\),
\[H_{Y'}^n(X',\mathcal O_{X'})\simeq H^{n-1}(U',\mathcal O_{U'}),\]
now \(H^{n-1}(U',\mathcal O_{U'})=0\), since \(U'\) is affine and we have \(n-1\geq1\), whence a contradiction.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.2.6.1.

Without the separation hypothesis on \(X\), (2.2.6) for an affine open \(U\) of \(X\) would be false, as is shown by the example of the affine plane with the origin doubled (EGA I 5.5.11).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.7.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian, separated, locally factorial scheme (i.e. one whose local rings are factorial). Then \(S\) is divisorial (2.2.5).
In particular (EGA IV 21.11.1):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2.7.1.

Every separated regular noetherian scheme is divisorial.

Proof of 2.2.7. We may assume \(S\) integral. Let \((S_i)_{i\in I}\) be a finite covering of \(S\) by nonempty affine opens. By virtue of (2.2.6), \(S-S_i\) is purely of codimension 1, hence, by (EGA IV 21.7.4), is the support of a positive divisor. In other words, for each \(i\in I\), there exists an invertible sheaf \(L_i\) and a section \(f_i\in\Gamma(S,L_i)\) such that \(S_i=S_{f_i}\). The family \((L_i)\) is then ample (2.2.4) by \(2.2.3 (i')\). This completes the proof.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.2.7.2.

As in (2.2.6), the separation hypothesis on \(S\) is essential. Indeed, let us take once again for \(S\) the affine plane with the origin doubled. Recall that \(S\) is obtained by gluing two copies of the affine plane \(\mathbf E_k^2\) (\(k\), a field) via the identity on the complement \(U\) of the origin \(0\); we thus have a canonical projection

\[p:S\longrightarrow\mathbf E_k^2,\]

which induces an isomorphism over \(U\), \(p^{-1}(0)\) consisting of two points \(0_1\) and \(0_2\). An invertible sheaf on \(S\) is obtained by gluing two invertible sheaves on \(\mathbf E_k^2\) via an isomorphism over \(U\); since every invertible sheaf on \(\mathbf E_k^2\) is trivial, an invertible sheaf on \(S\) is thus defined by an element \(g\in\Gamma(U,\mathcal O_U^*)\); but \(g\) is necessarily a constant, since the restriction \(\Gamma(\mathbf E_k^2,\mathcal O_{\mathbf E_k^2})\longrightarrow\Gamma(U,\mathcal O_U)\) is an isomorphism, \(0\) being of depth 2 in the plane; hence every invertible sheaf on \(S\) is trivial. It is immediate on the other hand that \(p\) induces an isomorphism

\[p^*:\Gamma(\mathbf E_k^2,\mathcal O_{\mathbf E_k^2})\longrightarrow\Gamma(S,\mathcal O_S).\]

Consequently, every global section of \(\mathcal O_S\) which vanishes at \(0_1\) also vanishes at \(0_2\), so the \(S_f\), for \(f\in\Gamma(S,\mathcal O_S)\), do not form a basis of the topology of \(S\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2.8.

Let \(S\) be a divisorial scheme (2.2.5), \((L_i)_{i\in I}\) an ample family of invertible \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules. Denote by \(\underline L(S)\) the strictly full additive subcategory of \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\) (2.2.1) generated by the \(L_i^{\otimes(-n)}\) for \((i,n)\in I\times\mathbb N^+\). Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\).

a) The categories \(\underline L(S)\) and \(\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\) (2.0) are quasi-liftable in \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\) (I 1.2) and quasi-stable under kernels of epimorphisms (I 1.2).

b) Let \(n\in\mathbb Z\). For \(E\) to be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent) (2.2.1) it is necessary and sufficient that \(E\) be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent) relative to \(\underline L(S)\) or \(\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\) (I 2.3). The canonical functors

\[K^-(\underline L(S))/K^{-,\varnothing}(\underline L(S))\longrightarrow D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\] \[\text{et}\qquad K^-(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))/K^{-,\varnothing}(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\]

are fully faithful and have essential image \(D^-(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (2.2.1).

c) Let \([m,n]\) be an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). For \(E\) to be of perfect amplitude contained in \([m,n]\) (2.2.1), it is necessary and sufficient that \(E\) be isomorphic, in \(D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\), to a complex \(F\) such that \(F^i=0\) for \(i\notin[m,n]\), \(F^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline L(S)\) for \(i>m\), with \(F^m\) locally free of finite type. The canonical functor

\[K^b(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))/K^{b,\varnothing}(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\]

is fully faithful and has essential image \(D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))_{\mathrm{parf}}\) (2.2.1).

Proof. Let \(F\longrightarrow G\) be an epimorphism of \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\), where \(G\) is of finite type. Since \(F\) is an inductive limit of its quasi-coherent subsheaves of finite type (EGA I 9.4.9), there exists, by virtue of (2.1.1 a)), a quasi-coherent subsheaf of finite type \(F'\) of \(F\) such that the composite \(F'\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow G\) is an epimorphism. By (2.2.3 (ii)), there exists an epimorphism \(E\longrightarrow F'\), with \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\underline L(S)\), so the composite \(E\longrightarrow F'\longrightarrow F\), composed with \(F\longrightarrow G\), is an epimorphism, which proves that the category of quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules of finite type is quasi-liftable in \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\), hence a fortiori the same holds for the category \(\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\) or \(\underline L(S)\). This proves the first assertion of a). For the second, let us apply (1.1), taking for \(\underline S\) the Zariski site of \(S\), for \(\underline C\) (resp. \(\underline C_0\)) the fibered category \(U\longmapsto\operatorname{Qcoh}(U)\) (resp. \(\underline L(U)\)) (\(U\) denotes a variable open of \(S\) and \(\underline L(U)\) the strictly full additive subcategory of \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(U)\) generated by the \(L_i^{\otimes(-n)}|U\)). Since every object of \(\underline L\) is pseudo-coherent, the notions of \(n\)-pseudo-coherence (resp. pseudo-coherence) (2.2.1) in the fibered category \(D(\operatorname{Qcoh})\) coincide with the same notions relative to \(\underline L\) (I 2.14). Condition (ii) (non-resp. case) of (1.1) being satisfied by virtue of (2.2.3 (ii)), we deduce from this, thanks to (1.1 a)), the second part of a) concerning \(\underline L(S)\); the assertion concerning \(\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\) is proved analogously. Assertions b) follow immediately from (1.1 b) and c)), taking into account the fact that if \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, then \(E\) is an object of \(D^-(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\) since \(S\) is quasi-compact. An object of the fibered category \(\operatorname{Qcoh}\) is locally in \(\underline L\) if and only if it is locally free of finite type: the category \(\underline L\) is therefore locally liftable in \(\operatorname{Qcoh}\) and locally stable under kernels of epimorphisms. Consequently, assertions c) follow from (1.2) applied for \(\underline C=\operatorname{Qcoh}\), \(\underline C_0=\underline L\), \(\underline C_{1S}=\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\). This completes the proof.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.9.

The hypotheses and notations are those of (2.2.8), but we assume moreover that \(S\) is separated or noetherian.

a) The canonical functors

\[K^{-,b}(\underline L(S))/K^{-,\varnothing}(\underline L(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\] \[\text{et}\qquad K^{-,b}(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))/K^{-,\varnothing}(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\]

are fully faithful and have essential image \(D^b(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (I 2.15). Moreover, if \(S\) is of "finite cohomological dimension" in the sense of (3.7)(1) (for example if the space underlying \(S\) is noetherian and of finite Krull dimension), then the functors

\[K^-(\underline L(S))/K^{-,\varnothing}(\underline L(S))\longrightarrow K^-(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))/K^{-,\varnothing}(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D^-(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\]

are equivalences of categories.

b) The canonical functor

\[K^b(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))/K^{b,\varnothing}(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\]

is fully faithful and has essential image \(D(S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) (I 4.9). For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(S)\) to be of perfect amplitude contained in \([m,n]\) (I 4.8), it is necessary and sufficient that \(E\) be isomorphic, in \(D(S)\), to a complex \(F\) such that \(F^i=0\) for \(i\notin[m,n]\), \(F^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline L(S)\) for \(i>m\), with \(F^m\) locally free of finite type.

Proof. Taking (3.5) and (3.7)(1) into account, this is an immediate corollary of (2.2.8): the only point to verify, which moreover follows trivially from (3.5) (resp. (3.7)), is that for an object \(E\) of \(D^b(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\) (resp. \(D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\) in the case where \(S\) is of finite cohomological dimension in the sense of (3.7)), the notions of pseudo-coherence and perfection of (2.2.1) coincide with the usual notions (I 2.15 and 4.9) when \(E\) is regarded as an object of \(D(S)\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.2.10.

a) If \(S\) is an affine scheme with ring \(A\), the family reduced to the sheaf \(\mathcal O_S\) is ample. For it, we have \(\underline L(S)=\operatorname{Lib}(S)\). It is moreover clear that the categories \(K^{-,\varnothing}(\operatorname{Lib}(S))\), and \(K^{-,\varnothing}(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\) are reduced to the zero objects. Consequently, we conclude from (2.2.9) that, if we denote by \(\operatorname{Mod}(A)\) the category of \(A\)-modules and \(D(A)=D(\operatorname{Mod}(A))\), the functor \(\mathbf R\Gamma_S:D^+(S)\longrightarrow D^+(A)\) induces a fully faithful functor \(D^b(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\longrightarrow D^b(A)\) (resp. \(D(S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\longrightarrow D(A)\)) whose essential image is the triangulated subcategory \(D^b(A)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (resp. \(D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\)) formed by the complexes with bounded cohomology which are pseudo-coherent (resp. perfect) relative to the subcategory \(\operatorname{Lib}(A)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Loclib}(A)\)) of free (resp. projective) modules of finite type. We have commutative diagrams of equivalences of categories

\[\xymatrix{ K^{-,b}(\operatorname{Lib}(S))\ar[r]\ar[d]_{\Gamma_S}&K^{-,b}(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\ar[r]\ar[d]_{\Gamma_S}&D^b(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\ar[d]^{\mathbf R\Gamma_S}\\ K^{-,b}(\operatorname{Lib}(A))\ar[r]&K^{-,b}(\operatorname{Loclib}(A))\ar[r]&D^b(A)_{\mathrm{coh}} }\]
\[\xymatrix{ K^b(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\ar[r]\ar[d]_{\Gamma_S}&D(S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\ar[d]^{\mathbf R\Gamma_S}\\ K^b(\operatorname{Loclib}(A))\ar[r]&D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}} }\]

(where, in the upper diagram, one may replace \(b\) by \(-\) when \(S\) is of finite cohomological dimension in the sense of (3.7)).

b) Let \(S\) be a scheme. It follows from a) that a complex of \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules which is pseudo-coherent and has bounded cohomology is locally isomorphic (in \(D(S)\)) to a strictly pseudo-coherent complex, which was by no means obvious a priori (cf. (I 2.3.1)).
2.3. Soft sheaves

Let us recall some definitions and results from [T.F]. Let \(X\) be a paracompact space. We say that a sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X\) is soft if, for every closed subset \(Y\) of \(X\), every section of \(F\) over \(Y\) extends to a section of \(F\) over \(X\). Soft abelian sheaves are acyclic, i.e. if \(F\) is soft, we have \(H^i(X,F)=0\) for \(i>0\) (T.F. II 4.4.3).

Let \(A\) be a sheaf of rings on \(X\). If \(F\) is a soft \(A\)-Module, \(F\) is generated by its sections (cf. 2.1.1): it is indeed clear that the homomorphism \(A_X^{(I)}\longrightarrow F\) defined by \(I=H^0(X,F)\) is an epimorphism.

On the other hand, if \(A\) is soft, every \(A\)-Module is soft (T.F II 3.7.1). As examples of soft sheaves of rings, let us mention the sheaf of continuous (real or complex) functions on a paracompact space, the sheaf of \(C^r\) functions on a manifold of class \(C^r\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3.2.

Let \(S\) be a compact space, equipped with a soft sheaf of rings \(A\). We denote by \(\operatorname{Mod}(S)\) the category of left \(A\)-Modules, \(D(S)=D(\operatorname{Mod}(S))\).

a) The category \(\operatorname{Lib}(S)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\)) (2.0) is liftable in \(\operatorname{Mod}(S)\) and quasi-stable (resp. stable) under kernels of epimorphisms (I 1.2)(1).

b) Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(S)\) and \(n\in\mathbb Z\). For \(E\) to be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent) it is necessary and sufficient that \(E\) be so relative to \(\operatorname{Lib}(S)\) or to \(\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\). The canonical functors

\[K^-(\operatorname{Lib}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\qquad\text{et}\qquad K^-(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\]

are fully faithful and have essential image \(D^-(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (I 2.15).

c) Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(S)\), and \([m,n]\) an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). For \(E\) to be of perfect amplitude contained in \([m,n]\) (4.8) it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a quasi-isomorphism \(F\longrightarrow E\), where \(F\) is a complex such that \(F^i=0\) for \(i\notin[m,n]\), \(F^i\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Lib}(S)\) for \(i>m\) and \(F^m\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\). The canonical functor

\[K^b(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\]

is fully faithful and has essential image \(D(S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) (4.9).

d) Denote by \(\operatorname{Mod}(A(S))\) the category of left \(A(S)\)-modules, \(D(A(S))\) the corresponding derived category. The functor

\[H^0(S,\cdot):\operatorname{Mod}(S)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(A(S))\]

is exact and an equivalence of categories. Moreover, it defines equivalences of categories:

\[D(S)\longrightarrow D(A(S)),\qquad D^-(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\longrightarrow D^-(A(S))_{\mathrm{coh}},\qquad D(S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\longrightarrow D(A(S))_{\mathrm{parf}},\]

the notions of pseudo-coherence (resp. perfection) in \(D(A(S))\) being understood relative to the category of free (resp. projective) modules of finite type. It also defines an equivalence of the full subcategory of \(D(S)\) formed by the \(n\)-pseudo-coherent objects (resp. objects of perfect amplitude contained in \([m,n]\)) onto the analogous full subcategory of \(D(A(S))\).

Proof. The first assertion of a) follows from (2.1.2 a)) applied by taking for \(\underline S\) the topos of sheaves of sets on \(S\), and \(\underline C'_S=\operatorname{Mod}(S)\) (taking (2.3.1) into account). The second follows from (1.1 a)) (resp. (1.2 a))) with \(\underline C=\operatorname{Mod}\) (fibered category of \(A\)-Modules on variable opens), \(\underline C_0=\operatorname{Lib}\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Loclib}\)), condition (ii) of (1.1) being satisfied by (2.3.1) and (2.1.1 b)). For assertion b), we apply (1.1 b), c)) (with \(\underline C\) and \(\underline C_0\) as before). Assertion c) follows from (1.2.1 b), c), d)), where \(\underline C=\operatorname{Mod}\) and \(\underline C_0=\operatorname{Lib}\). The first assertion of d) follows, thanks to (2.3.1), from (2.1.2 b) (ii)) where we take \(\underline C'_S=\underline C_S=\operatorname{Mod}(S)\). The other assertions of d) are deduced from the preceding one, taking into account b) and c), and the fact that the functor \(\Gamma_S\) transforms free Modules (resp. direct factors of free Modules) of finite type into free (resp. projective of finite type) modules.
2.4. Stein spaces

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.4.1.

Let \(\underline S\) be a locally ringed topos (I 2.15.1 (iii)), with final object \(S\). We say that \(\underline S\) is Stein if the structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_S\) is coherent (I 3.1) and if we have the following properties:

"Theorem A": every coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module (I 3.1) is generated by its sections,

"Theorem B": for every coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module \(F\), we have \(H^i(S,F)=0\) for \(i>0\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.4.1.1.

If \(\underline S\) has "enough closed points"(1) \(s\) such that the Ideal \(\mathfrak m_s\) defined by \(s\)(2) is coherent, then the relation "\(H^1(S,F)=0\) for every coherent sheaf \(F\)" implies Theorem A. Indeed, let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module and \(s\) a closed point of \(\underline S\). We have the exact sequence

\[0\longrightarrow\mathfrak m_sF\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow F/\mathfrak m_sF\longrightarrow0,\]

where \(F/\mathfrak m_sF\) is a coherent sheaf concentrated on \(s\). Applying the functor \(H^0(S,\cdot)\) and writing the cohomology exact sequence, we find that \(H^0(S,F)\longrightarrow H^0(S,F/\mathfrak m_sF)\) is an epimorphism. Since we have \(H^0(S,F/\mathfrak m_sF)\simeq F_s/\mathfrak m_sF_s\), where \(\mathfrak m_s\) is the maximal ideal of \(\mathcal O_{S,s}\), we conclude by Nakayama.

The writer does not know whether the preceding implication is true in general (when one assumes only that \(\mathcal O_S\) is a coherent sheaf of local rings).

The writer likewise does not know whether the relation "\(H^1(S,F)=0\) for every coherent sheaf \(F\)" implies Theorem B.

blocks.title: Examples
blocks.html:
Examples 2.4.2.

A complex analytic Stein space [3], a convex compact subset of \(\mathbb C^n\) equipped with the sheaf induced by the sheaf of holomorphic functions [3], the spectrum of a noetherian ring, an affine rigid analytic space [13] define Stein topoi.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4.3.

Let \(\underline S\) be a Stein topos (2.4.1). We assume the final object \(S\) is quasi-compact (2.1.1), and we denote by \(\operatorname{Coh}(S)\) the abelian category of coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules, i.e. those of finite presentation. Then:

a) The category \(\operatorname{Lib}(S)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\)) (2.0) is liftable in \(\operatorname{Coh}(S)\) and quasi-stable (resp. stable) under kernels of epimorphisms (I 1.2).

b) The canonical functors

\[K^-(\operatorname{Lib}(S))\longrightarrow K^-(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D^-(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\]

are equivalences of categories.
c) Set \(A=\Gamma(S,\mathcal O_S)\), and denote by \(\operatorname{Mod}(A)\) the category of \(A\)-modules, \(D(A)\) the corresponding derived category. The functor
\[\Gamma_S:\operatorname{Coh}(S)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(A)\]

is exact, fully faithful and has as essential image the strictly full additive subcategory of \(\operatorname{Mod}(A)\) formed by the \(A\)-modules of finite presentation. It induces a fully faithful functor
\[D^-(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\longrightarrow D(A),\]
whose essential image is the full subcategory \(D^-(A)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) formed by the pseudo-coherent complexes (relative to the subcategory of free modules of finite type).

Proof. For the first assertion of a) we apply (2.1.2 a)) for \(\underline C'_S=\operatorname{Coh}(S)\), the hypothesis of (2.1.2) being satisfied by Theorem B: with the notations of (2.1.2 a)), if \(E\) is coherent and \(G\) locally free of finite type, then \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(G,E)\) is coherent, hence the category \(\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\) (and a fortiori \(\operatorname{Lib}(S)\)) is liftable in \(\operatorname{Coh}(S)\). The second assertion of a) follows from (1.1 a)) (resp. (1.2 a))) applied for \(\underline C_0=\operatorname{Lib}\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Loclib}\))(1), the condition (ii) (resp. case) of (1.1) being satisfied by Theorem A and (2.1.1 b)). Assertion b) follows from (1.1 c)) applied successively for \(\underline C_0=\operatorname{Lib}\) and \(\underline C_0=\operatorname{Loclib}\). The first assertion of c) follows from (2.1.2 b) (iii)) applied for \(\underline C'_S=\operatorname{Coh}(S)\). To prove that the functor \(\Gamma_S:D^-(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\longrightarrow D(A)\) is fully faithful, it suffices, taking b) into account, to prove that the composite
\[K^-(\operatorname{Lib}(S))\longrightarrow D^-(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\xrightarrow{\Gamma_S}D(A)\]
is so. But the latter factors as
\[K^-(\operatorname{Lib}(S))\xrightarrow{(*)}K^-(\operatorname{Lib}(A))\xrightarrow{(**)}D(A),\]

where \(\operatorname{Lib}(A)\) denotes the category of free \(A\)-modules of finite type and \((*)\) is induced by \(\Gamma_S\). It is trivial that \((*)\) is an equivalence of categories. On the other hand, it follows from (I 2.7 b)) applied for \(\underline C=\operatorname{Mod}(A)\), \(\underline C_0=\operatorname{Lib}(A)\) (and taking into account the fact that \(K^{-,\varnothing}(\operatorname{Lib}(A))=0\)) that \((**)\) is fully faithful and has essential image \(D^-(A)_{\mathrm{coh}}\). This proves the last assertion of c) and completes the proof of (2.4.3).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4.4.

Under the hypotheses of (2.4.3), the canonical functor

\[D^b(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\]

is fully faithful and has essential image \(D^b(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (I 2.15).

Proof. We apply (2.1.3) with \(A=\operatorname{Coh}(S)\), \(B=\operatorname{Mod}(S)\), \(\phi:A\longrightarrow B\) being the inclusion functor. The latter being fully faithful, it suffices to verify, by (2.1.3 a) (ii)), that for fixed \(M\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Coh}(S)\) and \(n>0\) the functor \(L\longmapsto\operatorname{Ext}_{\operatorname{Mod}(S)}^n(L,M)\) is co-effaceable at \(L\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Coh}(S)\). Now, by Theorem A and (2.1.1 b)), every coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module is a quotient of a free Module of finite type, and, if \(L\) is free of finite type, we have \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\operatorname{Mod}(S)}^n(L,M)=0\) by Theorem B, whence our assertion. We deduce that the functor \(D^b(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\) is fully faithful and has as essential image the full subcategory of \(D(S)\) formed by the complexes with bounded coherent cohomology, which is nothing other than \(D^b(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) since \(\mathcal O_S\) is coherent (I 3.5 b)).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4.5.

With the hypotheses and notations of (2.4.3), we have:

a) The canonical functors

\[K^{-,b}(\operatorname{Lib}(S))\longrightarrow K^{-,b}(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D^b(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\]

are equivalences of categories. Moreover the functor \(\mathbf R\Gamma_S:D^+(S)\longrightarrow D^+(A)\) induces a fully faithful functor

\[\mathbf R\Gamma_S:D^b(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\longrightarrow D^+(A),\]

whose essential image is \(D^b(A)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (triangulated subcategory of \(D(A)\) formed by the complexes with bounded cohomology which are pseudo-coherent relative to the category of free modules of finite type).

b) Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(S)\), and let \([m,n]\) be an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). If \(E\) is of perfect amplitude contained in \([m,n]\) (I 4.8), then \(E\) is isomorphic to a complex \(F\) such that \(F^i=0\) for \(i\notin[m,n]\), \(F^i\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Lib}(S)\) for \(i>m\) and \(F^m\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\).

c) The canonical functor

\[K^b(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\quad\text{(I 4.9)}\]

is an equivalence of categories. Moreover, the functor \(\mathbf R\Gamma_S:D^+(S)\longrightarrow D^+(A)\) induces a fully faithful functor

\[\mathbf R\Gamma_S:D(S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\longrightarrow D^+(A),\]

whose essential image is \(D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) (triangulated subcategory of \(D(A)\) formed by the perfect complexes relative to the category of projective modules of finite type).
Proof. a) The functors
\[K^{-,b}(\operatorname{Lib}(S))\longrightarrow K^{-,b}(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D^b(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\xrightarrow{\Gamma_S}D^b(A)_{\mathrm{coh}}\]
are equivalences of categories by virtue of (2.4.3 b) and c)). Since the (fully faithful) functor \(\Gamma_S:D^b(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\longrightarrow D(A)\) factors as
\[D^b(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\longrightarrow D^b(S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\xrightarrow{\mathbf R\Gamma_S}D(A),\]
we conclude thanks to (2.4.4).

b) Suppose \(\operatorname{parf.amp}(E)\subset[m,n]\). In particular, \(E\) is pseudo-coherent; by virtue of a), we may therefore assume, replacing \(E\) if necessary by an isomorphic object, that \(E^i\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Lib}(S)\) for \(i>m\) and \(E^i=0\) for \(i>n\). Truncating if necessary, we may even assume that \(E^i=0\) for \(i<m\). By (I 4.16) applied for \(\underline C=\operatorname{Mod}\), \(\underline C_0=\operatorname{Lib}\), we then have \(E^m\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\), whence the assertion.

c) Hypothesis (1.1 (i)) (resp. (1.1 (ii) resp. case)) being satisfied for \(\underline C_S=\operatorname{Coh}(S)\), \(\underline C_0=\operatorname{Lib}\) by (2.4.3 a)) (resp. (2.1.1 b)) and Theorem A), we know by (1.2 c) and d)) that the functor
\[K^b(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D^b(\operatorname{Coh}(S))\]

is fully faithful. Hence, by (2.4.4), the same holds for the functor \(K^b(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\), whose essential image is \(D(S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) by virtue of b). By an argument analogous to the one made in the proof of (2.4.3 c)), we see that the functor \(K^b(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\) induced by \(\Gamma_S\) is fully faithful and has essential image \(D(S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), and we complete the proof as in a).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.4.6.

The writer does not know whether in (2.4.4) one can replace \(D^b\) by \(D^-\), and consequently whether (2.4.5 a)) is still true when one replaces \(K^{-,b}\) by \(K^-\) and \(D^b\) by \(D^-\).
To finish, here is an interesting complement to (2.4.5). Let us first give a definition:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.4.7.

We call a complex pseudo-analytic space any ringed space locally isomorphic to a ringed space of the type \((Y,\mathcal O_X|Y)\), where \(X\) is a complex analytic space, and \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\).

The structure sheaf of a complex pseudo-analytic space is therefore coherent.

We say that a complex pseudo-analytic space is Stein if the corresponding topos is (2.4.1).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4.8.(1)

Let \(S\) be a complex pseudo-analytic space, compact, and Stein (2.4.7). We assume that, for every open \(U\) of \(S\) and every reduced closed subspace \(X\) of \(U\), the normalization of \(X\) is locally connected(2) (a condition satisfied for example if \(S\) is defined locally by real analytic equations or inequations in a space \(\mathbb R^n\) [15]). Then the ring \(A=\Gamma(S,\mathcal O_S)\) is a noetherian ring.

Proof. The point is to show that, if \(M\) is an \(A\)-module of finite presentation, the set of submodules (or, what amounts to the same, the set of submodules of finite type) of \(M\) satisfies the maximal condition. Now, if \(N\) is a submodule of finite type of \(M\), then \(M/N\) is of finite presentation by (I 2.6 and 2.9), hence \(N\) is of finite presentation by the equivalence (2.4.3 c)). Using again the equivalence (2.4.3 c)) between the category of coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules and the category of \(A\)-modules of finite presentation, and the compactness of \(S\), we are reduced to proving the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4.8.1.

Let \(S\) be a complex pseudo-analytic space satisfying the local connectedness condition of (2.4.8). Let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module, \((E_n)\) an increasing sequence of coherent sub-\(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules of \(F\), \(s\) a point of \(S\). Then there exists an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(s\) such that the sequence \((E_n|U)\) is stationary.

Proof. The ring \(\mathcal O_{S,s}\) being noetherian, there exists an integer \(n_0\) such that \((E_n)_s=(E_{n_0})_s\) for \(n\geq n_0\). Replacing the sequence \((E_n)\) if necessary by the sequence \((E_n/E_{n_0})\) for \(n\geq n_0\), we may therefore assume that \((E_n)_s=0\) for every \(n\).
On the other hand, \(F_s=M\) is an \(\mathcal O_{S,s}\)-module of finite type, so there exists (Bourbaki Alg. com. chap. IV §1 th.1) a finite filtration
\[M=M_0\supset M_1\supset\cdots\supset M_q=0\]

such that \(M_i/M_{i+1}\simeq\mathcal O_{S,s}/\mathfrak p_i\), where \(\mathfrak p_i\) is a prime ideal of \(\mathcal O_{S,s}\). Since the category of \(\mathcal O_{S,s}\)-modules of finite type is equivalent to the category of germs at \(s\) of coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules, we may assume, localizing if necessary in a neighborhood of \(s\), that the \(M_i\) (resp. \(\mathfrak p_i\)) are the fibers at \(s\) of coherent sub-\(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules \(F_i\) (resp. coherent Ideals \(P_i\)) of \(F\) (resp. \(\mathcal O_S\)) such that we have
\[F=F_0\supset F_1\supset\cdots\supset F_q=0\]

and \(F_i/F_{i+1}\simeq\mathcal O_S/P_i\). Proving that the sequence \((E_n)\) is stationary in a neighborhood of \(s\) is obviously equivalent to proving that the sequences induced on each step of the filtration \((F_i)\) of \(F\) are. So we may assume that \(F=\mathcal O_S\), and that \(\mathcal O_{S,s}\) is an integral ring, the \(E_n\) forming an increasing sequence of Ideals of \(\mathcal O_S\) such that \((E_n)_s=0\). Since \(\mathcal O_{S,s}\) is integral, hence in particular reduced, we may moreover assume, thanks to Oka's theorem ([10] IV §3), localizing at \(s\) if necessary, that \(S\) is reduced. Let us show that there exists an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(s\) such that \(E_n|U=0\) for \(n\) large enough. For this, consider the normalization \(\widetilde S\) of \(S\) ([10] IV §4). We know (loc. cit.) that \(\widetilde S\) is the analytic spectrum ([10] II §3) of a finite \(\mathcal O_S\)-Algebra \(\widetilde{\mathcal O}_S\) whose fiber at each \(t\in S\) is the integral closure of \(\mathcal O_{S,t}\) (cf. note (2) of (2.4.8)). We have \(E_n\subset E_n\widetilde{\mathcal O}_S\), so it suffices to prove that \(E_n\widetilde{\mathcal O}_S=0\) for \(n\) large enough. Now we have ([10] II §3 Prop. 8)
\[E_n\widetilde{\mathcal O}_S=p_*\bigl(\widetilde{E_n\widetilde{\mathcal O}_S}\bigr),\]

where \(p:\widetilde S\longrightarrow S\) is the canonical projection, and \(\widetilde{E_n\widetilde{\mathcal O}_S}\) the Ideal of \(\mathcal O_{\widetilde S}\) canonically defined by \(E_n\widetilde{\mathcal O}_S\). It therefore suffices to show that there exists an open neighborhood \(V\) of \(s\) in \(\widetilde S\) such that \(\widetilde{E_n\widetilde{\mathcal O}_S}|V=0\) for \(n\) large enough, which reduces us to the case where \(S\) is normal, which we shall now assume. Since \((E_n)_s=0\), the closed immersion \(i_n:S_n\longrightarrow S\) defined by \(E_n\) is étale at \(s\). But, \(S\) being normal, the set of points where \(i_n\) is étale is both open and closed (see the lemma below), so its image in \(S\) is a subset of \(S\) which is both open and closed. Hence the restriction of \(E_n\) to the connected component of \(s\) in \(S\) is zero; this connected component being an open set, since \(S\) is locally connected, we have won. It thus remains to establish the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4.8.2. (cf. EGA IV 18.10.3)

Let \(Y\) be a normal pseudo-analytic space(1), and \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) an unramified morphism. The set of points of \(X\) where \(f\) is étale is both open and closed.

Proof. It is immediate that the set of points where \(f\) is étale is open (for saying that \(f\) is étale at a point means that \(f\) is an isomorphism in a neighborhood of that point ([8] VI)). It remains to show that the set of points where \(f\) is not étale is open. Since an unramified morphism is locally an immersion ([8] VI), we may assume that \(f\) is a closed immersion, defined by a coherent Ideal \(I\). Let \(x\) be a point of \(X\) such that \(I_x\neq0\). Let \(\mathfrak p\) be a prime ideal of \(\mathcal O_{Y,x}\) containing \(I_x\). We have \(\dim(\mathcal O_{Y,x}/\mathfrak p)>0\), for otherwise we would have \(\mathfrak p=0\) since \(\mathcal O_{Y,x}\) is integral. Hence \(X\) is of codimension \(>0\) at \(x\), hence of codimension \(>0\) in a neighborhood of \(x\) ([10] III A Cor. 1 of lemma 3), and we win.

footnotes.html: Hypothesis satisfied if \(\underline S\) is locally of finite type (SGA 4 VI 1.2) or if \(\underline S\) is the topos of sheaves of sets on a compact space and \(S\) the final object (T.F II 3.10.1).
This result, which is incidental, will not be used in the rest of the Seminar: the reader is advised to skip it on first reading.
This notion, in the form \(i'\), was introduced by M. Borelli and S. L. Kleiman; the terminology "ample family" is proposed by the writer.
§ 3 is independent of the rest of the exposé.
This assertion holds more generally for \(S\) paracompact.

i.e. enough fiber functors (SGA 4 IV 6.4.1) of the type \(i_s:s\longrightarrow\underline S\), where \(i_s\) is the inclusion of a closed subset of \(\underline S\).
(2) i.e. formed by the sections of \(\mathcal O_S\) whose fiber at \(s\) belongs to the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m_s\) of \(\mathcal O_{S,s}\).
\(\operatorname{Lib}\) and \(\operatorname{Loclib}\) are locally stable under kernels of epimorphisms by (I 2.15.1).

This result, as well as its proof, was graciously communicated to the writer by A. Grothendieck; it was obtained independently by J. Frisch [5].
(2) We shall take for granted that the notions of local analytic Geometry of [10] extend to complex pseudo-analytic spaces; the reduction to the usual case is indeed immediate in each instance.

pages.title: 3. Complements on quasi-coherent sheaves on schemes(2)

blocks.html: 3. Complements on quasi-coherent sheaves on schemes(2)
In this section, we fix a quasi-compact quasi-separated scheme \(S\). The canonical (fully faithful) inclusion functor
\[\phi:\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(S)\tag{3}\]
extends to a functor, still denoted \(\phi\), from \(D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\) to \(D(S)=D(\operatorname{Mod}(S))\), which we propose to study(4).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1.

The category \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\) is an abelian category satisfying (AB 5) and for which the quasi-coherent sheaves of finite type form a "small" family of generators. In particular (Tohoku 1.10.1), \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\) has enough injectives.

Proof. The assertion concerning generators follows at once from the fact that every quasi-coherent sheaf on \(S\) is a filtered inductive limit of its quasi-coherent subsheaves of finite type (EGA I 9.4.9 and IV 1.7.7). The other assertions are obvious.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.1.1.

The writer does not know whether \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\) still has enough injectives when one assumes only that \(S\) is quasi-separated.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.2.

The functor \(\phi:\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(S)\) has a right adjoint

\[Q:\operatorname{Mod}(S)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Qcoh}(S),\]

(called the coherator).

Proof. If \(S\) is affine, the existence of \(Q\) is immediate: one takes \(Q=\widetilde{\Gamma_S}\) (EGA ErrI 1.8.7). Now let \(f:U\longrightarrow S\) be an affine open of \(S\); it is clear that, for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\) and \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Mod}(U)\), we have a canonical isomorphism
\[(1)\qquad\operatorname{Hom}(E,f_*F)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}(E,f_*Q(F)).\]

Let then \((f_i:U_i\longrightarrow S)\) be a finite covering of \(S\) by affine opens, and for each \((i,j)\) let \((U_{ijk}\longrightarrow U_{ij})\) be a finite covering of \(U_{ij}=U_i\cap U_j\) by affine opens (\(U_{ij}\) is quasi-compact since \(S\) is quasi-separated). Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Mod}(S)\). We have an exact sequence
\[(2)\qquad0\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow\prod_i f_{i*}(F|U_i)\longrightarrow\prod_{ijk}f_{ijk*}(F|U_{ijk}),\]

(where \(f_{ijk}\) denotes the inclusion of \(U_{ijk}\) into \(S\)). Note that, if \(g:V\longrightarrow U\) is an inclusion of affine opens of \(S\), we have an obvious natural arrow \(Q(F|U)\longrightarrow g_*Q(F|V)\). Consequently, we deduce from (2) an arrow
\[(3)\qquad\prod_i f_{i*}Q(F|U_i)\longrightarrow\prod_{ijk}f_{ijk*}Q(F|U_{ijk}),\]

whose kernel we shall denote by \(QF\). Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\). Applying the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(E,\cdot)\) to the sequence (2), we obtain, taking (1) into account, an isomorphism functorial in \(E\):
\[\operatorname{Hom}(E,F)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}(E,QF).\]
This proves the existence of \(Q\); uniqueness is obvious since it is an adjoint.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3.

a) The coherator is an additive functor transforming injectives into injectives.

b) Denote by

\[\mathbf RQ:D^+(S)\longrightarrow D^+(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\]

the right derived functor of the coherator. For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\), and \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(S)\), there exist canonical functorial isomorphisms

\[(i)\quad\mathbf R\operatorname{Hom}_{\operatorname{Mod}}(E,F)\simeq\mathbf R\operatorname{Hom}_{\operatorname{Qcoh}}(E,\mathbf RQ(F)),\] \[(ii)\quad\operatorname{Hom}_{\operatorname{Mod}}(E,F)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}_{\operatorname{Qcoh}}(E,\mathbf RQ(F)).\]

Proof. Assertion a) is an immediate consequence of the definition. For b), it suffices to establish the isomorphism (i), since (ii) will be deduced from it by passing to cohomology. For this, we may assume \(F\) has injective components; \(QF\) then has injective components by a), and the isomorphism (i) comes simply from the definition of \(Q\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.4.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of quasi-compact quasi-separated schemes. For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Mod}(X)\), there exists a canonical functorial isomorphism

\[Qf_*(E)\simeq f_*Q(E).\]

Proof. Since \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated (EGA IV 1.2.3 and 1.2.4), \(f_*\) transforms quasi-coherent sheaves into quasi-coherent sheaves (III 1.4.10 and IV 1.7.21). The functors \(Qf_*\) and \(f_*Q\) from \(\operatorname{Mod}(X)\) to \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(Y)\) are both right adjoints of the functor \(f^*:\operatorname{Qcoh}(Y)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(X)\), hence canonically isomorphic.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.5.

If \(S\) is noetherian, or quasi-compact and separated, the functor

\[\phi:D^+(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\longrightarrow D^+(S)\tag{3.0}\]

is fully faithful and has as essential image the full subcategory \(D^+(S)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) of \(D^+(S)\) formed by the complexes with quasi-coherent cohomology.

Proof. We may restrict ourselves to proving the assertion for the case where \(S\) is separated, the case where \(S\) is noetherian being well known ([H] III 7.19). It obviously amounts to the same to prove the following assertions:
(3.5.1): for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\), the adjunction arrow
\[E\longrightarrow\mathbf RQ(E)\]
(deduced from (3.3 b) (ii))) is an isomorphism;
(3.5.2): for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(S)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\), the adjunction arrow
\[\mathbf RQ(E)\longrightarrow E\]
(deduced from (3.3 b) (ii))) is an isomorphism.

Proof of (3.5.1). By an immediate dévissage(1), we are reduced to proving that quasi-coherent sheaves are acyclic for the coherator, (i.e. that, for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\), we have \(R^iQ(E)=0\) for \(i>0\)), and that on the other hand, for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\), the adjunction arrow \(E\longrightarrow QE\) is an isomorphism. If \(S\) is affine, this follows from the formula \(Q=\widetilde{\Gamma_S}\) (cf. proof of (3.2)). In the general case, let us cover \(S\) by a finite number of affine opens \(U_i\). Since \(S\) is separated, the inclusions \(f_i:U_i\longrightarrow S\) are affine morphisms, as moreover are the inclusions \(f_{i_0\ldots i_p}:U_{i_0\ldots i_p}=U_{i_0}\cap\cdots\cap U_{i_p}\longrightarrow S\). Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\), and consider the resolution of \(E\) defined by the covering \((U_i)\) (T.F. II 5.2):
\[(*)\qquad0\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow\prod_i f_{i*}f_i^*(E)\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow\prod_{i_0\ldots i_p}f_{i_0\ldots i_p*}f_{i_0\ldots i_p}^*(E)\longrightarrow\cdots\]
It provides a spectral sequence
\[(**)\qquad E_1^{pq}=\prod_{i_0\ldots i_p}R^qQ\bigl(f_{i_0\ldots i_p*}f_{i_0\ldots i_p}^*(E)\bigr)\Longrightarrow R^{p+q}Q(E).\]
Since \(f_{i_0\ldots i_p}\) is affine, we have by (3.4):
\[(***)\qquad R^qQ\bigl(f_{i_0\ldots i_p*}f_{i_0\ldots i_p}^*(E)\bigr)\simeq f_{i_0\ldots i_p*}R^qQ\bigl(f_{i_0\ldots i_p}^*(E)\bigr)\]
for every \(q\), whence in particular \(E_1^{pq}=0\) for \(q>0\), by the affine case, already treated. From \((**)\) we deduce isomorphisms
\[R^nQ(E)\simeq H^n(QE^\bullet)\quad\text{pour }n\geq0,\]
where \(E^\bullet\) is the resolution of \(E\) defined by the exact sequence \((*)\). But by \((***)\) we have \(QE^n\simeq E^n\) for \(n\geq0\), whence finally
\[R^nQ(E)=0\quad\text{pour }n>0,\qquad Q(E)\simeq E,\]
qed.
Proof of (3.5.2). By the spectral sequence
\[R^pQ(H^q(E))\Longrightarrow R^{p+q}Q(E),\]
we reduce at once to the case where \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\). The composite
\[E\longrightarrow\mathbf RQ(E)\longrightarrow E\]
of the adjunction arrows being the identity, the assertion thus follows from (3.5.1). This completes the proof of (3.5).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.6.

The conclusion of (3.5) would be false if one omitted the hypothesis "separated or noetherian", as is shown by a pretty counterexample of J. L. Verdier (see Appendix I to the present exposé).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.7.

Under the hypothesis of (3.5), suppose moreover that there exists an integer \(N\) such that we have, for every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Mod}(S)\) and every quasi-compact open \(U\) of \(S\),

\[H^i(U,F)=0\quad\text{pour }i>N\tag{1}.\]

Then:

a) The coherator is of finite cohomological dimension.

b) The functor

\[\phi:D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\]

is fully faithful and has as essential image the full subcategory \(D(S)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) of \(D(S)\) formed by the complexes with quasi-coherent cohomology.

Proof of a). If \(S\) is affine, the formula \(Q=\widetilde{\Gamma_S}\) (cf. proof of (3.2)) implies that \(Q\) is of cohomological dimension \(\leq N\). Let us pass to the general case. Let us show that there exists an integer \(a\) such that we have \(R^qQ(E)=0\) for every \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Mod}(S)\) and every \(q>a\). The hypothesis implies that every \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Mod}(S)\) admits a finite right resolution \(E^i\) of length \(N\) such that the \(E^i\) are acyclic, i.e. satisfy \(H^q(U,E^i)=0\) for every open \(U\) of \(S\) and every \(q>0\). Consequently, it suffices to prove that there exists an integer \(b\) such that \(R^qQ(E)=0\) for every acyclic \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module \(E\) and every \(q>b\). (One may then take \(a=b+N\)). Let \((U_i)\) be a finite covering of \(S\) by \(N'\) affine opens. Let \(E\) be an acyclic \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module, and consider the resolution \(C^\bullet(E)\) of \(E\) defined by the covering \((U_i)\) (T.F. II 5.2). Two cases are to be examined:

1°) \(S\) is separated. Then the components of \(C^\bullet(E)\) are sums of terms of the type \(f_*f^*E\), where \(f:U\longrightarrow S\) is the inclusion of an affine open (hence an affine morphism, \(S\) being separated). We have
\[\mathbf R(Qf_*)f^*E\simeq\mathbf RQ\,\mathbf Rf_*f^*E\simeq\mathbf RQ\,f_*f^*E,\quad\text{puisque }f^*E\text{ est acyclique}.\]
On the other hand, by (3.4), we have
\[\mathbf R(Qf_*)f^*E\simeq\mathbf R(f_*Q)f^*E\simeq f_*\mathbf RQ(f^*E),\quad\text{puisque }f\text{ est affine}.\]
We deduce from this
\[R^nQ(f_*f^*E)\simeq f_*R^nQ(f^*E)\quad\text{pour tout }n>0,\]
whence, by the affine case, \(R^nQ(f_*f^*E)=0\) for \(n>N\). Since \(C^\bullet(E)\) is of length \(N'\), we finally have
\[R^nQ(E)=0\quad\text{pour }n>N+N',\]
and we have won.

2°) \(S\) is noetherian. The components of \(C^\bullet(E)\) are sums of terms of the type \(f_*f^*E\), where \(f:U\longrightarrow S\) is the inclusion of a quasi-compact separated open. As in case 1°), we have
\[(*)\qquad\mathbf R(Qf_*)f^*E\simeq\mathbf RQ\,f_*f^*E\simeq\mathbf R(f_*Q)f^*E.\]

Since \(U\) is noetherian, \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(U)\) has enough injective objects in \(\operatorname{Mod}(U)\) ([H] II 7.18), so the right derived functor of \(f_*:\operatorname{Qcoh}(U)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\) is "induced" by \(\mathbf Rf_*:D^+(U)\longrightarrow D^+(S)\) when one identifies \(D^+(\operatorname{Qcoh}(U))\) (resp. \(D^+(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\)) with a full subcategory of \(D^+(U)\) (resp. \(D^+(S)\)) thanks to (3.5). Consequently, we have
\[\mathbf R(f_*Q)f^*E\simeq\mathbf Rf_*\,\mathbf RQ(f^*E).\]
We deduce from this a spectral sequence
\[E_2^{pq}=R^pf_*R^qQ(f^*E)\Longrightarrow R^{p+q}(f_*Q)f^*E.\]

By case 1°), there exists an integer \(n(U)\) depending only on \(U\) such that \(Q\) is of cohomological dimension \(\leq n(U)\) on \(\operatorname{Mod}(U)\). We therefore have \(E_2^{pq}=0\) for \(q>n(U)\). But we have \(E_2^{pq}=0\) for \(p>N\), because of the "cohomological dimension" hypothesis on \(S\). We therefore have
\[R^n(f_*Q)f^*E=0\quad\text{pour }n>N+N'',\]
where \(N''\) is an integer greater than all the \(n(U)\), with \(U\) running through the set of intersections \(U_{i_0}\cap\cdots\cap U_{i_p}\). Taking \((*)\) into account, we obtain
\[R^nQ(f_*f^*E)=0\quad\text{pour }n>N+N'',\]
whence finally
\[R^nQ(E)=0\quad\text{pour }n>N+N'+N'',\]
which completes the proof of a).
Proof of b). The coherator, being of finite cohomological dimension by a), admits ([H] I 5.3) a right derived functor
\[\mathbf RQ:D(S)\longrightarrow D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\]
(whose restriction to \(D^+(S)\) coincides with the functor \(\mathbf RQ\) defined above). For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\), there exists a canonical functorial isomorphism
\[(1)\qquad E\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathbf RQ(E),\]
since, by (3.5.1), the \(E^i\) are acyclic for \(Q\) and such that \(E^i\xrightarrow{\sim}Q(E^i)\). On the other hand, for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(S)\), we have a canonical functorial homomorphism
\[(2)\qquad\mathbf RQ(E)\longrightarrow E\]

(coming, in the case where the \(E^i\) are \(Q\)-acyclic, from the ordinary adjunction homomorphism between \(\phi\) and \(Q\)). One trivially verifies that the homomorphisms (1) and (2) make \(\mathbf RQ:D(S)\longrightarrow D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\) a right adjoint of \(\phi:D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\longrightarrow D(S)\). The fact that (1) is an isomorphism implies that \(\phi\) is fully faithful. Finally, using the spectral sequence
\[R^pQ(H^q(E))\Longrightarrow R^{p+q}Q(E),\]

(which converges since \(Q\) is of finite cohomological dimension), we see that (2) is an isomorphism for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(S)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\), hence that \(\phi\) has essential image \(D(S)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\). This proves b) and completes the proof of (3.7).

footnotes.html:
It suffices that the local rings of \(Y\) be integral.
(2) This section is due essentially to J. L. Verdier.
(3) \(\operatorname{Mod}(S)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\)) denotes the category of \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules (resp. quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules).
(4) We shall omit writing \(\phi\) in formulas where there is no risk of confusion.
The index \(\operatorname{Mod}\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Qcoh}\)) is an abbreviation for \(D(S)\) (resp. \(D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\)).
Using for example the spectral sequence \(R^pQ(H^q(E))\Longrightarrow R^{p+q}Q(E)\).
Condition satisfied, by (Tohoku 3.6.5), if the space underlying \(S\) is noetherian and of Krull dimension \(\leq N\).

blocks.html:
APPENDIX I
A COUNTEREXAMPLE OF VERDIER(1)
0. Introduction

If \(S\) is a scheme, we denote by \(\operatorname{Mod}(S)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\)) the category of \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules (resp. quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules). The object of this appendix is to draw the reader's attention to certain pathological properties of the category \(D(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\), and in particular to prove the following facts:
0.1.

Let \(X\) be an affine scheme with ring \(A\), and \(I\) an injective \(A\)-module. It is known ([H] II 7.14) that, if \(A\) is noetherian, the sheaf \(\widetilde I\) is an injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module. If \(A\) is not noetherian, \(\widetilde I\) is not necessarily injective, nor even flasque, even if the space underlying \(X\) is noetherian and of finite Krull dimension. Moreover, the restriction of \(\widetilde I\) to an open \(U\) of \(X\) is not in general an injective of \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(U)\).
0.2.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of quasi-compact quasi-separated schemes. The functor \(\mathbf Rf_*:D^+(X)\longrightarrow D^+(Y)\) does not in general induce on \(D^+(\operatorname{Qcoh}(X))\) (a full subcategory of \(D^+(X)\) by (II 3.5)) the right derived functor of \(f_*:\operatorname{Qcoh}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(Y)\). More precisely, an injective of \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(X)\) is not necessarily acyclic for \(f_*:\operatorname{Mod}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(Y)\).
0.3.

Let \(S\) be a quasi-compact quasi-separated scheme. An object of \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S)\), even an injective one, is not necessarily acyclic for the coherator (II 3.2). The functor \(\phi:D^b(\operatorname{Qcoh}(S))\longrightarrow D^b(S)\) (II 3.0) is not in general fully faithful.
1. The counterexamples
Let us first recall the following lemma (SGA 2 II 9):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.

Let \(A\) be a ring, \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\mathbf f=(f_\alpha)\) a finite system of elements of \(A\), \(Y\) the closed set defined by \(\mathbf f(x)=0\). For \(i>0\), the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) for every injective \(A\)-module \(I\), we have \(H_Y^i(X,\widetilde I)=0\);

(ii) the pro-object \(n\longmapsto H_i(\mathbf f^n,A)\) is zero, i.e. for every \(n\), there exists \(n'>n\) such that \(H_i(\mathbf f^{n'},A)\longrightarrow H_i(\mathbf f^n,A)\) is zero.
1.2.

We are going to use (1.1) to prove (0.1). Let \(k\) be a field, \(B=k[[x,y]]\), \(\mathbf f\) the system \((x,y)\), \(M=\prod_{n\geq0}B/(\mathbf f^n)\), \(A\) the \(B\)-algebra \(D_B(M)\) (EGA 0IV 18.2.3). We trivially have
\[H_2^A(\mathbf f^n,A)\simeq H_2^B(\mathbf f^n,A).\tag{1}\]
On the other hand we have
\[H_2^B(\mathbf f^n,A)\simeq H_2^B(\mathbf f^n,B)\oplus H_2^B(\mathbf f^n,M),\]
but \(H_2^B(\mathbf f^n,B)=0\) since \(\mathbf f\) is a regular sequence (EGA III 1.1.4 and 0IV 15.1.20), so we have
\[H_2^B(\mathbf f^n,A)\simeq H_2^B(\mathbf f^n,M).\]
Finally we have (EGA II 1.1.3.3)
\[H_2^B(\mathbf f^n,M)\simeq H_B^0(\mathbf f^n,M),\]
\(H_B^0(\mathbf f^n,M)\) being canonically identified with the submodule \((\mathbf f^n)_M\) of \(M\) annihilated by \((\mathbf f^n)\). Whence finally:
\[(*)\qquad H_2^A(\mathbf f^n,A)\simeq(\mathbf f^n)_M.\]

Since the transition morphism \(H_2^A(\mathbf f^{n'},A)\longrightarrow H_2^A(\mathbf f^n,A)\) is induced by multiplication by \(\mathbf f^{,n'-n}\) (EGA III 1.1.6), one immediately verifies, with the help of \((*)\), that the pro-object \(n\longmapsto H_2^A(\mathbf f^n,A)\) is not zero. We then deduce from (1.1) that there exists an injective \(A\)-module \(I\) such that \(H_Y^2(X,\widetilde I)\neq0\), hence such that \(\widetilde I\) is not flasque (SGA 2 I 2.12). Let us remark in passing that the projection \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(B)\) is a homeomorphism, hence that the space underlying \(X\) is noetherian of dimension 2. Let \(I\) be an injective \(A\)-module such that \(H_Y^2(X,\widetilde I)\neq0\), and denote by \(U\) the open complement of \(Y\) in \(X\). We have (SGA 2 II 4)
\[H_Y^2(X,\widetilde I)\simeq H^1(U,\widetilde I)\neq0.\]

Now, since \(U\) is separated, the functor \(D^+(\operatorname{Qcoh}(U))\longrightarrow D^+(U)\) is fully faithful (II 3.5), so \(\widetilde I|U\) is not injective in \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(U)\). We have thus proved assertion (0.1).

Denote by \(Z\) the quasi-compact quasi-separated scheme obtained by gluing two copies of \(X\) along \(U\), \(j_1,j_2\) the two canonical open immersions of \(X\) into \(Z\), so that we have a cartesian square
\[\xymatrix{ U\ar[r]^i\ar[d]_i&X\ar[d]^{j_1}\\ X\ar[r]_{j_2}&Z. }\]

We have \(j_2^*R^1j_{1*}(\widetilde I)\simeq R^1i_*(i^*\widetilde I)\), whence \(H^0(X,R^1j_{1*}(\widetilde I))\simeq H^1(U,\widetilde I)\neq0\), hence \(R^1j_{1*}(\widetilde I)\neq0\), which proves (0.2).
On the other hand, we have, by (II 3.4 and 3.5),
\[\mathbf RQ\,\mathbf Rj_{1*}(\widetilde I)\simeq j_{1*}(\widetilde I).\]
We deduce from this a spectral sequence, whose exact sequence of low-degree terms provides the isomorphism
\[R^1j_{1*}(\widetilde I)\simeq R^2Q(j_{1*}\widetilde I).\]
The sheaf \(j_{1*}\widetilde I\) being injective in \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(Z)\), we have proved the first part of (0.3).
Finally, let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Qcoh}(Z)\). We have the duality isomorphism
\[\mathbf R\operatorname{Hom}_{\operatorname{Mod}}(E,Rj_{1*}(\widetilde I))\simeq\mathbf R\operatorname{Hom}_{\operatorname{Mod}}(j_1^*E,\widetilde I),\]
but since \(X\) is affine, we have (II 3.5)
\[\mathbf R\operatorname{Hom}_{\operatorname{Mod}}(j_1^*E,\widetilde I)\simeq\mathbf R\operatorname{Hom}_{\operatorname{Qcoh}}(j_1^*E,\widetilde I)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}(j_1^*E,\widetilde I).\]
We deduce from this
\[\mathbf R\operatorname{Hom}_{\operatorname{Mod}}(E,Rj_{1*}(\widetilde I))\simeq\operatorname{Hom}(j_1^*E,\widetilde I),\]
whence a spectral sequence, whose exact sequence of low-degree terms gives the isomorphism
\[\operatorname{Ext}_{\operatorname{Mod}}^2(E,j_{1*}\widetilde I)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}(E,R^1j_{1*}\widetilde I).\]

Consequently, the identity morphism of \(E=R^1j_{1*}(\widetilde I)\) provides a nonzero element of \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\operatorname{Mod}}^2(E,j_{1*}\widetilde I)\), so the functor \(D^b(\operatorname{Qcoh}(Z))\longrightarrow D^b(Z)\) is not fully faithful, which completes the proof of (0.3).

footnotes.html:
