This appendix reproduces, up to a few editorial details, two letters from J.-L. Verdier (30/9/67 and 11/10/67) to the writer, in answer to a question of the latter.
The exponent \(A\) or \(B\) indicates relative to which ring the Koszul complex is computed.

pages.title: APPENDIX IIDEFINITION OF THE ANALYTIC INDEX OF A RELATIVE ELLIPTIC COMPLEX

blocks.html:
APPENDIX II
DEFINITION OF THE ANALYTIC INDEX OF A RELATIVE ELLIPTIC COMPLEX

Our aim, in this appendix, is to show that the notion of perfect complex leads to a simple and natural definition of the "analytic index" of a continuous family of elliptic differential operators, or more generally of a "relative elliptic complex". For the initiated reader, let us say that the definition given here has the advantage over the classical definitions (see for example [2] [17]) of not requiring any deformation of the elliptic complex under consideration.

§§ 1 and 2 consist of recollections on the classical notion of "mixed structure"(1). We give, of course, only the minimum of definitions and results required to be able to state and prove, in § 3, the central theorem we have in view, which says, a little more precisely, that the direct image, under the projection (assumed proper) onto the base (assumed locally compact), of a relative elliptic complex is a perfect complex. When the base \(S\) is compact, the definition of the analytic index as an element of \(K(S)\) (the "Grothendieck group" of complex vector bundles of finite rank on \(S\)) then follows from the globalization sorite of (II 2.3.2).
In all that follows, if \(S\) is a topological space, we shall denote by \(\mathcal O_S\) the sheaf of continuous real-valued functions on \(S\).
1. The sorite of mixed structures
In this section, we fix a topological space \(S\).
1.1. Definition of mixed manifolds

Let \(V\) be an open of \(\mathbb R^n\). For \(r\in\mathbb N\), we denote by \(C^r(V)\) the Fréchet space of real-valued functions of class \(C^r\) on \(V\). We set \(C^\infty(V)=\varprojlim_r C^r(V)\).
Let \(U\) be an open of \(S\). For \(r\in\mathbb N\cup\{\infty\}\), we denote by \({}^r\!\mathcal O_{U\times V}\) the sheaf of rings on \(U\times V\) defined by
\[\Gamma(U'\times V',{}^r\!\mathcal O_{U'\times V'})=C(U',C^r(V')),\]
where \(U'\) (resp. \(V'\)) is an open of \(U\) (resp. \(V\)), and \(C(U',C^r(V'))\) denotes the space of continuous maps from \(U'\) to \(C^r(V')\). We have a canonical injection
\[\operatorname{pr}_1^{-1}(\mathcal O_U)\longrightarrow{}^r\!\mathcal O_{U\times V},\]
which makes \((U\times V,{}^r\!\mathcal O_{U\times V})\) a ringed space over \(U\).

We say that a ringed space \(X\) over \(S\) is a mixed manifold of class \(C^r\) (\(r\in\mathbb N\cup\{\infty\}\)) if \(X\) is locally isomorphic (as a ringed space over \(S\)) to a space of the type \((U\times V,{}^r\!\mathcal O_{U\times V})\). (If \(S\) is reduced to a point, a mixed manifold of class \(C^r\) over \(S\) is nothing other than a manifold of class \(C^r\) in the usual sense).

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) be a mixed manifold of class \(C^r\). For \(s\in S\), the fiber \(X_s=f^{-1}(s)\), equipped with the sheaf \(i_s^{-1}(\mathcal O_X)/I_s\), where \(i_s:X_s\longrightarrow X\) is the injection and \(I_s\) the ideal of sections which "vanish" on \(X_s\), is a manifold of class \(C^r\).
1.2. "Relative" differential operators

It is easy to carry out, on the model of Algebraic Geometry (EGA IV 16, SGA 3 VII), an infinitesimal study of mixed manifolds. We shall confine ourselves to reviewing quickly the notions we shall need.
1.2.1.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) be a mixed manifold of class \(C^\infty\) (1.1). For every \(n\in\mathbb N\), we define the sheaf \(P^n_{X/S}\) of principal parts of order \(n\) on \(X\). In two different ways, corresponding to the two projections \(\operatorname{pr}_1\) and \(\operatorname{pr}_2\) of \(X\times_S X\) onto \(X\), it is an \(\mathcal O_X\)-Algebra augmented toward \(\mathcal O_X\), locally free of finite type as an \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module. When we do not specify the \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module structure considered on \(P^n_{X/S}\), it will be understood that it is the one coming from \(\operatorname{pr}_1\). We have canonical isomorphisms
\[P^0_{X/S}\simeq\mathcal O_X,\qquad P^1_{X/S}\simeq\mathcal O_X\oplus\Omega^1_{X/S},\]

where \(\Omega^1_{X/S}\) is the sheaf of relative differentials of \(X\) with respect to \(S\), or relative cotangent sheaf. We denote by \(T_{X/S}\) the dual of \(\Omega^1_{X/S}\), which is called the relative tangent sheaf. The sheaf \(T_{X/S}\) (resp. \(\Omega^1_{X/S}\)) is the sheaf of sections of a vector bundle of finite rank on \(X\), called the relative tangent (resp. cotangent) bundle, which is a mixed \(S\)-manifold over \(X\).
The \(f^{-1}(\mathcal O_S)\)-linear homomorphism (corresponding to the \(\mathcal O_X\)-Algebra structure defined by \(\operatorname{pr}_2\))
\[d^n_{X/S}:\mathcal O_X\longrightarrow P^n_{X/S},\]
which to a section of \(\mathcal O_X\) associates its principal part of order \(n\), is called the universal differential operator of order \(n\) on \(X\) relative to \(S\).
1.2.2.

More generally, if \(E\) is a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module of finite type, one can consider the sheaf \(P^n_{X/S}(E)=P^n_{X/S}\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}E\) of principal parts of order \(n\) of \(E\) (the tensor product being defined by means of the second \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module structure on \(P^n_{X/S}\)), and we have a universal differential operator
\[d^n_{X/S}(E):E\longrightarrow P^n_{X/S}(E).\]

Let \(E,F\) be locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules of finite type. By definition, a differential operator from \(E\) to \(F\) of order \(\leq n\) is an \(f^{-1}(\mathcal O_S)\)-linear homomorphism from \(E\) to \(F\) which factors through \(d^n_{X/S}(E)\) as an \(\mathcal O_X\)-linear homomorphism from \(P^n_{X/S}(E)\) to \(F\) (the factorization is then unique). In other words, the operator \(d^n_{X/S}(E)\) establishes a bijection
\[(1.2.2.1)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal O_X}(P^n_{X/S}(E),F)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Diff}^n(E,F),\]
where \(\operatorname{Diff}^n(E,F)\) denotes the set of differential operators from \(E\) to \(F\) of order \(\leq n\).
1.2.3.

We shall also have to consider differential operators with complex coefficients. Denote by \(\mathcal O_S\otimes\mathbb C\) (resp. \(\mathcal O_X\otimes\mathbb C\)) the complexification of \(\mathcal O_S\) (resp. \(\mathcal O_X\)). Let \(E,F\) be locally free \((\mathcal O_X\otimes\mathbb C)\)-Modules of finite type. By a differential operator from \(E\) to \(F\) of order \(\leq n\), we shall mean an \(f^{-1}(\mathcal O_S\otimes\mathbb C)\)-linear homomorphism from \(E\) to \(F\) which factors through \(d^n_{X/S}(E)\) as an \((\mathcal O_X\otimes\mathbb C)\)-linear homomorphism from \(P^n_{X/S}(E)\) to \(F\). If we denote by \(\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^n(E,F)\) the set of these operators, the operator \(d^n_{X/S}(E)\) thus defines a bijection
\[(1.2.3.1)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal O_X\otimes\mathbb C}(P^n_{X/S}(E),F)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^n(E,F).\]
1.2.4.
For \(n\geq1\), we have a canonical exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow S^n(\Omega^1_{X/S})\longrightarrow P^n_{X/S}\longrightarrow P^{n-1}_{X/S}\longrightarrow0.\]

Whence, tensoring over \(\mathcal O_X\)(2) with a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module (resp. \((\mathcal O_X\otimes\mathbb C)\)-Module) of finite type \(E\), an exact sequence of \(\mathcal O_X\) (resp. \((\mathcal O_X\otimes\mathbb C)\))-Modules:
\[(1.2.4.1)\qquad 0\longrightarrow S^n(\Omega^1_{X/S})\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}E\longrightarrow P^n_{X/S}(E)\longrightarrow P^{n-1}_{X/S}(E)\longrightarrow0.\]

Let \(F\) be a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\) (resp. \((\mathcal O_X\otimes\mathbb C)\))-Module of finite type. Applying to (1.2.4.1) the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal O_X}(\mathord{\cdot},F)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal O_X\otimes\mathbb C}(\mathord{\cdot},F)\)), we deduce from it, taking into account the identifications (1.2.2.1) and (1.2.4.1), an exact sequence

\[(1.2.4.2)\qquad 0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Diff}^{n-1}(E,F)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Diff}^n(E,F)\xrightarrow{\sigma_n}\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal O_X}(S^n(\Omega^1_{X/S})\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}E,F)\]
(resp.

\[0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^{n-1}(E,F)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^n(E,F)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal O_X\otimes\mathbb C}(S^n(\Omega^1_{X/S})\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}E,F)).\]

If \(X\) is paracompact and \(\mathcal O_X\) soft (cf. II 2.3.1), one can complete the exact sequence (1.2.4.2) by adding a zero on the right. The homomorphism \(\sigma_n\) is called the symbol; it associates to a differential operator from \(E\) to \(F\) of order \(\leq n\) a "homogeneous polynomial map of degree \(n\) from \(\Omega^1_{X/S}\) to \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal O_X}(E,F)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal O_X\otimes\mathbb C}(E,F)\))", which one can also regard, if one wishes, as a homomorphism \(p^*(E)\longrightarrow p^*(F)\) (where \(p\) is the projection onto \(X\) of the relative cotangent bundle (1.2.1)), satisfying a certain homogeneity condition.

Let \(E,F,G\) be locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules of finite type, and \(u\in\operatorname{Diff}^m(E,F)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Diff}^n(F,G)\), whence \(vu\in\operatorname{Diff}^{m+n}(E,G)\). One easily verifies the relation
\[(1.2.4.3)\qquad \sigma_{m+n}(vu)=\sigma_n(v)\,\sigma_m(u).\]
(The same relation holds for differential operators with complex coefficients). In particular, if the composite of two differential operators is zero, the composite of their symbols is as well.
1.2.5.

Let \(E\) be a complex, in bounded degrees, of differential operators with complex coefficients on \(X\) relative to \(S\) (1.2.3), with \(d^i:E^i\longrightarrow E^{i+1}\) of order \(\leq n_i\). By (1.2.4.3), we have
\[\sigma_{n_{i+1}}(d^{i+1})\,\sigma_{n_i}(d^i)=0.\]

We say that \(E\) is an elliptic complex of order \(\mathbf n=(n_i)\) if the complex \((p^*(E^i),\sigma_{n_i}(d^i))\) (where \(p\) is the projection onto \(X\) of the cotangent bundle) is acyclic outside the zero section.
When \(S\) is reduced to a point, the preceding notion of elliptic complex on \(X\) coincides with the usual notion, studied in detail in [4] or [16].
1.2.6.
Let \(s\in S\), \(i_s\) be the inclusion of the fiber \(X_s\) into \(X\) (regarded as a monomorphism of ringed spaces) cf. (1.1). For \(n\in\mathbb N\), we have a canonical isomorphism
\[i_s^*(P^n_{X/S})\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}P^n_{X_s},\]
whence a restriction homomorphism
\[i_s^*:\operatorname{Diff}^n(E,F)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Diff}^n(i_s^*(E),i_s^*(F)),\]

for \(E,F\) locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules of finite type (and an analogous homomorphism for operators with complex coefficients). Let \(E\) be a complex, in bounded degrees, of differential operators with complex coefficients on \(X\); for \(E\) to be elliptic (1.2.5) it is necessary and sufficient that, for every \(s\in S\), \(i_s^*(E)\) be an elliptic complex (verification left to the reader).

footnotes.html: The knowledgeable reader is advised to skip them.

\(S^n(\ )\) denotes the \(n\)-th symmetric power.
(2) For the second \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module structure of \(P^n_{X/S}\) and \(P^{n-1}_{X/S}\).

blocks.html: 2. Rigidity lemmas
2.0.

It is "well known" that compact differentiable manifolds are "rigid", i.e. admit no continuous deformation other than the trivial one. However, since this result will serve us in an essential way in § 3, and since references seem rather scarce, it is perhaps not useless to give precise statements and a few proofs.
We fix a topological space \(S\). The mixed manifolds in question will be assumed to be of class \(C^r\), with \(r\geq1\) (possibly \(r=\infty\)).
2.1. Notations.

Let \(X,Y\) be mixed \(S\)-manifolds. We denote by \(\operatorname{Hom}(X,Y)\) the set of \(S\)-morphisms from \(X\) to \(Y\), and by \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X,Y)\) the sheaf on \(X\) of germs of \(S\)-morphisms from \(X\) to \(Y\) (defined by
\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X,Y)(U)=\operatorname{Hom}(U,Y)\]
for \(U\) an open of \(X\)).
Let \(s\in S\). We denote by \(i_s\) the inclusion of \(X_s\) into \(X\). We have a canonical induction homomorphism
\[(2.1.1)\qquad i_s^{-1}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X,Y)\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X_s,Y_s)\]

which is an epimorphism, as one sees at once. In the case where \(Y=S\times\mathbb R\), the sheaf \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X,Y)\) is canonically identified with \(\mathcal O_X\), and the epimorphism (2.1.1) with the epimorphism
\[i_s^{-1}(\mathcal O_X)\longrightarrow i_s^*(\mathcal O_X)=\mathcal O_{X_s}\]
considered in (1.1).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition(*) 2.2.

Let \(X,Y\) be mixed \(S\)-manifolds, and \(s\in S\). We assume that the fiber \(X_s\) is paracompact and that \(s\) admits a fundamental system of paracompact neighborhoods. Let \(A\) be a closed subset of \(X_s\), and \(f\in H^0(A,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X_s,Y_s))\). The subsheaf \(F\) of \(i_s^{-1}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X,Y)\) formed by the germs "which extend \(f\)" (i.e. whose image under (2.1.1) coincides with \(f\) over \(A\)) is soft.

Proof. Let \(X'\) be an open neighborhood of \(x\) in \(X\), isomorphic (as a mixed manifold) to \(S'\times U\), where \(S'\) is a paracompact neighborhood of \(s\) and \(U\) a relatively compact open of \(\mathbb R^m\), and such that the image under \(f\) of \(X'_s\cap A\) is contained in an open of \(Y_s\) isomorphic to \(S'\times V\), where \(V\) is a convex open of \(\mathbb R^n\) containing the origin. By (T.F. II 3.4.1), it suffices to show that every section of \(F\) over a closed subset \(A_0\) of \(X_s\) contained in \(X'_s\) extends to \(X'_s\). Now \(X'_s\;(\simeq\{s\}\times U)\) is relatively compact, so that a closed subset of \(X_s\) contained in \(X'_s\) is compact. Let us identify \(X'\) (resp. \(Y'\)) with \(S'\times U\) (resp. \(S'\times V\)), set \(A'=A\cap X'_s\), and let \(A_0\) be a compact subset of \(X'_s=\{s\}\times U\). Since \(A'\) is closed in \(\{s\}\times U\), which is paracompact, we have ([T.F.] II 3.3.1)(1)
\[H^0(A',\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X_s,Y_s))=\varinjlim_{U'\supset A'}\operatorname{Hom}(U',Y'_s),\]
over the opens \(U'\) of \(X'_s\) containing \(A'\). Likewise, since \(A_0\) is closed in \(S'\times U\), which is paracompact (product of a paracompact space and a compact space), we have
\[H^0(A_0,i_s^{-1}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X,Y))=\varinjlim_{U_0\supset A_0}\operatorname{Hom}(U_0,Y'),\]
over the opens \(U_0\) of \(X'\) containing \(A_0\). The proposition will thus be a consequence of the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2.1.

Let \(U\) be an open of \(\mathbb R^m\), \(V\) a convex open of \(\mathbb R^n\) containing \(0\). Let \(A_0,A_1\) be closed subsets of \(\{s\}\times U\), and let \(U_0\) (resp. \(U_1\)) be an open of \(S\times U\) (resp. \(\{s\}\times U\)) containing \(A_0\) (resp. \(A_1\)). Let \(f_1:U_1\longrightarrow V\) be a morphism and let \(f_0:U_0\longrightarrow S\times V\) be a \(S\)-morphism inducing on \(U_0\cap(\{s\}\times U)\) a morphism defining the same germ as \(f_1\) in a neighborhood of \(A_1\). Then there exists an open \(U'\) of \(S\times U\) containing \(\{s\}\times U\), and a \(S\)-morphism \(\bar f:U'\longrightarrow S\times V\) such that

(i) the germ of \(\bar f\) along \(\{s\}\times U\) extends the germ defined by \(f_0\);

(ii) the morphism induced by \(\bar f\) on \(\{s\}\times U\) defines the same germ as \(f_1\) in a neighborhood of \(A_1\).

Proof. Replacing \(f_1\) if necessary by \(af_1\), where \(a\) is a \(C^r\) function on \(U\), equal to 1 in a neighborhood of \(A_1\) and with support in \(U_1\), we may assume that \(U_1=U\). Let \(b\) be a \(C^r\) function on \(U\), equal to 1 on \(A_0\) and with support in \(U_0\cap(\{s\}\times U)\). Then
\[U'=U_0\cup(S\times(U-\operatorname{Supp}b))\]
is an open of \(S\times U\) containing \(\{s\}\times U\). The morphism \(\bar f:U'\longrightarrow S\times V\) defined by
\[\bar f(t,x)=(t,b(x)\operatorname{pr}_2f_0(t,x)+(1-b(x))f_1(x))\]
visibly satisfies conditions (i) and (ii).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3.

Under the hypotheses of (2.2), the sheaves \(i_s^{-1}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X,Y)\), \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X_s,Y_s)\) are soft. In particular, the sheaves \(i_s^{-1}(\mathcal O_X)\), \(\mathcal O_{X_s}\) are soft(1). For every closed subset \(A\) of \(X_s\), the map

\[H^0(X_s,i_s^{-1}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X,Y))\longrightarrow H^0(A,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X_s,Y_s))\]

deduced from (2.1.1) is surjective.

Proof. The fact that \(i_s^{-1}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X,Y)\) is soft follows from (2.2) applied for \(A=\varnothing\); taking \(S=s\), we deduce that \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X_s,Y_s)\) is soft. The last assertion follows from (2.2).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4.

Under the hypotheses of (2.2), let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module. For every closed subset \(A\) of \(X_s\), the canonical homomorphism

\[H^0(X_s,i_s^{-1}(E))\longrightarrow H^0(A,i_s^*(E)),\]

deduced from the epimorphism

\[i_s^{-1}(E)\longrightarrow i_s^*(E)=i_s^{-1}(E)\otimes_{i_s^{-1}(\mathcal O_X)}\mathcal O_{X_s},\]

is an epimorphism.

Proof. Since \(i_s^{-1}(\mathcal O_X)\) is soft (2.3), the functor \(H^0(A,\mathord\cdot)\) is exact on the category of \(i_s^{-1}(\mathcal O_X)\)-Modules (T.F. II 3.7.1, 3.4.2, 4.4.3), so the homomorphism
\[H^0(A,i_s^{-1}(E))\longrightarrow H^0(A,i_s^*(E))\]
is an epimorphism. Since \(i_s^{-1}(E)\) is soft, the homomorphism
\[H^0(X_s,i_s^{-1}(E))\longrightarrow H^0(A,i_s^{-1}(E))\]
is an epimorphism, and we win.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5.

Let \(p:X\longrightarrow S\) be a mixed manifold, and \(s\in S\). We assume that \(s\) admits a fundamental system of paracompact neighborhoods \(S'\) such that the \(p^{-1}(S')\) form a fundamental system of paracompact neighborhoods of \(X_s\) (a condition satisfied for example if \(S\) is locally compact and \(p\) a proper map of topological spaces). Then, for every \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(E\), we have

\[R^ip_*(E)_s=0\qquad\text{pour }i>0.\]
Proof. By (T.F. II 4.17.1), we have
\[R^ip_*(E)_s=H^i(X_s,i_s^{-1}(E)).\]
But, since \(i_s^{-1}(\mathcal O_X)\) is soft (2.3), we have
\[H^i(X_s,i_s^{-1}(E))=0\qquad\text{pour }i>0,\]
whence the assertion.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.6.

Let \(X,Y\) be mixed \(S\)-manifolds, and \(s\in S\). We assume that \(X\) satisfies the hypotheses of (2.5). For every morphism \(f:X_s\longrightarrow Y_s\), there exists an open neighborhood \(S'\) of \(s\) and a morphism \(\bar f:X|S'\longrightarrow Y|S'\) such that \(\bar f_s=f\).
Proof. An immediate consequence of (2.3) (with \(A=X_s\)) and of the fact that we have, thanks to the hypotheses on \(X\),
\[H^0(X_s,i_s^{-1}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X,Y))=\varinjlim_{S'\ni s}\operatorname{Hom}(X|S',Y)\qquad(S'\text{ ouvert})\]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.7.

Let \(X,Y\) be mixed \(S\)-manifolds, \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) a morphism, \(s\in S\), \(x\in X_s\). If the tangent linear map of \(f_s:X_s\longrightarrow Y_s\) is invertible at \(x\), then \(f\) is a local isomorphism at \(x\), i.e. there exist an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\) in \(X\) and an open \(V\) of \(Y\) such that \(f\) induces an isomorphism from \(U\) onto \(V\).
Proof. This is an easy consequence of the implicit function theorem. The details are left to the reader.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.8.

Let \(X,Y\) be mixed \(S\)-manifolds, and \(s\in S\). We assume that \(s\) admits a fundamental system of neighborhoods \(S'\) such that the \(X|S'\) (resp. \(Y|S'\)) form a fundamental system of neighborhoods of \(X_s\) (resp. \(Y_s\)) (a condition satisfied for example if \(S\) is locally compact and \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) proper over \(S\)). Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a \(S\)-morphism such that \(f_s:X_s\longrightarrow Y_s\) is an isomorphism. Then there exists a neighborhood \(S'\) of \(s\) such that \(f|X|S':X|S'\longrightarrow Y|S'\) is an isomorphism.

Proof. By (2.7), there exists an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(X_s\) in \(X\) such that \(f|U\) is étale (i.e. a local isomorphism). We may assume that \(U=X|S'\), where \(S'\) is a neighborhood of \(s\) in \(S\); in other words, shrinking \(S\) if necessary, we may assume that \(f\) is étale. One then easily verifies that the fiber product \(Z=X\times_YX\) is naturally equipped with a structure of mixed \(S\)-manifold (étale over \(X\) for each projection), and that the diagonal morphism \(\Delta:X\longrightarrow Z\) is an open immersion(1). It is immediate, on the other hand, that \(Z\) satisfies the same hypothesis as \(X\) and \(Y\), namely that the opens of the form \(Z|S'\), for \(S'\) an open neighborhood of \(s\) in \(S\), form a fundamental system of neighborhoods of \(Z_s\). Since \(\Delta_s:X_s\longrightarrow Z_s\) is an isomorphism, it follows, shrinking \(S\) if necessary, that \(\Delta\) is an isomorphism. Hence \(f\) is an open immersion, hence an isomorphism after shrinking \(S\) once more, and we have won.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.9.

Let \(X\) be a mixed \(S\)-manifold, and \(s\in S\). We assume the hypothesis of (2.5) is satisfied. Then there exists a neighborhood \(S'\) of \(s\) in \(S\) such that \(X|S'\) is \(S'\)-isomorphic to \(S'\times X_s\).
Proof. Apply (2.6) and (2.8).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.10.

Let \(s\in S\) and let \(V\) be a paracompact manifold. Set \(X=S\times V\), and denote by \(i_s\) the injection of \(V\) into \(X\) defined by \(s\). Suppose that the hypothesis of (2.5) is satisfied. Let \(E\) be a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\) (resp. \(\mathcal O_X\otimes\mathbb C\))-Module of finite type. Then there exists a neighborhood \(S'\) of \(s\) in \(S\) such that \(E|X|S'\) is isomorphic to \(\operatorname{pr}_2^*i_s^*(E)\) (\(\operatorname{pr}_2:S'\times V\longrightarrow V\) being the second projection).
Proof. Set \(F=\operatorname{pr}_2^*i_s^*(E)\). By virtue of (2.4) applied to the Module \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\), the induction homomorphism
\[\operatorname{Hom}(i_s^{-1}(E),i_s^{-1}(F))\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(i_s^*(E),i_s^*(F))\]
is an epimorphism. On the other hand, we have (T.F. II 3.3.1):

\[\begin{aligned} \operatorname{Hom}(i_s^{-1}(E),i_s^{-1}(F)) &\simeq H^0(X_s,i_s^{-1}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F))\\ &=\varinjlim_{S'\ni s}H^0(X|S',\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F))\quad(S'\text{ ouvert})\\ &\simeq\varinjlim_{S'\ni s}\operatorname{Hom}(E|X|S',F|X|S'). \end{aligned}\]

Hence the canonical isomorphism \(i_s^*(E)\simeq i_s^*(F)\) is induced by a homomorphism \(f:E\longrightarrow F\) (after shrinking \(S\) if necessary). Since \(f_s\) is an isomorphism, \(f\) is an isomorphism in a neighborhood of \(X_s\) by Nakayama, hence above \(X|S'\), where \(S'\) is a suitable neighborhood of \(s\), qed.

footnotes.html:
Proposition communicated to the editor by A. Douady.
(1) A closed subset of a paracompact space admits a fundamental system of paracompact neighborhoods.
(1) For \(\mathcal O_{X_s}\), this is "very well known". The editor does not know whether \(\mathcal O_X\) is soft when the hypotheses of (2.2) are satisfied for each \(s\in S\) and \(X\) is paracompact. This would trivially imply (under the said hypotheses) the softness of \(i_s^{-1}(\mathcal O_X)\) as well as (2.5) below.
Note the analogy with Algebraic Geometry (SGA 1 I 5.1).

blocks.html: 3. The finiteness theorem
3.0. Conventions and notation
3.0.1.
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. We denote by \(C(X,Y)\) the set of continuous maps from \(X\) to \(Y\), and by \(\underline C(X,Y)\) the sheaf on \(X\) defined by \(U\longmapsto C(U,Y)\).
3.0.2.
Let \(E,F\) be Banach spaces (real or complex). We denote by \(L(E,F)\) the Banach space of continuous linear maps from \(E\) to \(F\).
3.0.3.

The mixed manifolds considered in this section will be assumed to be of class \(C^\infty\). Let \(X\) be a mixed \(S\)-manifold; we shall often identify a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\) (resp. \(\mathcal O_X\otimes\mathbb C\))-Module of finite type with the corresponding real (resp. complex) vector bundle.
3.0.4.

Let \(X\) be a compact (ordinary) manifold, and let \(E\) be a (real or complex) vector bundle of finite rank on \(X\). We denote by \(C^\infty(X,E)\) the Fréchet space of sections (of class \(C^\infty\)) over \(X\) of \(E\). For \(r\in\mathbb Z\), we denote by \(C_2^r(X,E)\) the Hilbert space of sections of \(E\) that are \(r\) times differentiable in \(L^2\) ([16] IX). For \(s\geq r\), \(C_2^s(X,E)\) maps into \(C_2^r(X,E)\) by an injective continuous linear map with dense image, and we have (loc. cit.)
\[C^\infty(X,E)=\varprojlim_r C_2^r(X,E).\]
3.0.5.

Let \(S\) be a topological space. We shall need the notion of "Hilbert bundle" over \(S\), for which we refer the reader to [12] or [14]. There is an additive functor from the category of Hilbert bundles (real resp. complex) over \(S\) to that of \(\mathcal O_S\) resp. \(\mathcal O_S\otimes\mathbb C\)-Modules, associating with each Hilbert bundle \(E\) the sheaf \(\underline E\) of continuous sections of \(E\).
3.1. Auxiliary technical results

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1.1.

Let \(X,Y\) be topological spaces, with \(X\) locally compact. Denote by \(C'(X,Y)\) the presheaf defined on the base of relatively compact open subsets of \(X\) by \(C'(X,Y)(U)=C(\overline U,Y)\) (3.0.1). The canonical restriction morphism

\[C'(X,Y)\longrightarrow\underline C(X,Y)\]

induces an isomorphism on the associated sheaves.
Proof. This is immediate.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1.2.

Let \(E,F\) be locally convex topological vector spaces (over \(\mathbb C\), to fix ideas), and let \(u:E\longrightarrow F\) be a continuous (resp. continuous with dense image) linear map. Let \(X\) be a compact space. Equip \(C(X,E)\), \(C(X,F)\) with the topology of uniform convergence. Then the linear map

\[u_X:C(X,E)\longrightarrow C(X,F)\]

induced by \(u\) is continuous (resp. continuous with dense image).

Proof. It is clear that \(u_X\) is continuous. On the other hand (Bourbaki, Intégration, chap. III § 2, lemmas 1 and 2), the subspace \(C(X,\mathbb C)\otimes_{\mathbb C}E\) of \(C(X,E)\) (consisting of the continuous maps from \(X\) to \(E\) which take their values in a finite-dimensional vector subspace) is dense; likewise \(C(X,\mathbb C)\otimes_{\mathbb C}F\) is dense in \(C(X,F)\). Suppose \(u\) is continuous with dense image. Then
\[u_X(C(X,\mathbb C)\otimes_{\mathbb C}E)=C(X,\mathbb C)\otimes_{\mathbb C}u(E)\]
is dense in \(C(X,\mathbb C)\otimes_{\mathbb C}F\), whence we deduce, by taking closures, that \(u_X(C(X,E))\) is dense in \(C(X,F)\), qed.
3.1.3.

Let \(V\) be a compact manifold, \(E\) and \(F\) complex vector bundles(1) of finite rank on \(V\), and \(n\in\mathbb N\). The space \(\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^n(E,F)\), which is identified by (1.2.3.1) with
\[\operatorname{Hom}(P^n(E),F)\simeq C^\infty(V,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P^n(E),F)),\]

is naturally equipped with the structure of a Fréchet space. Let \(u\in\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^n(E,F)\); one easily checks that the linear map from \(C^\infty(V,E)\) to \(C^\infty(V,F)\) defined by \(u\) is continuous. If \(L(C^\infty(V,E),C^\infty(V,F))\) denotes the space of continuous linear maps from \(C^\infty(V,E)\) to \(C^\infty(V,F)\), we thus have a linear map
\[\phi:\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^n(E,F)\longrightarrow L(C^\infty(V,E),C^\infty(V,F)).\]
On the other hand, for \(r\in\mathbb Z\), \(u\) acts by extension of scalars ([16] IX Th. 12) on the sections \(C_2^r\) of \(E\) and defines a linear map
\[\phi_r:\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^n(E,F)\longrightarrow L(C_2^r(V,E),C_2^{r-n}(V,F)).\]
One easily checks that:
(i) the \(\phi_r\) are continuous (the target space is a Banach space (3.0.2));
(ii) \(\phi=\varprojlim_r\phi_r\) (i.e. for \(u\in\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^n(E,F)\), \(\phi(u)\) is the limit of the projective system of the \(\phi_r(u)\)).

The preceding assertions are easy starting from the results of (loc. cit.): one reduces at once to \(n=0\), (ii) then follows directly from the definitions, while (i) follows (after localizing the problem by means of a partition of unity) from the formula giving the successive derivatives of a product.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1.4.

Let \(S\) be a topological space, \(V\) a compact manifold, \(E\) and \(F\) complex vector bundles of finite rank on \(V\), and \(n\in\mathbb N\). Denote by \(X\) the mixed \(S\)-manifold \(S\times V\). For \(u\in\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^n(\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E),\operatorname{pr}_2^*(F))\), the map \(s\longmapsto i_s^*(u)\) from \(S\) to \(\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^n(E,F)\) is continuous. The linear map

\[\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^n(\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E),\operatorname{pr}_2^*(F))\longrightarrow C(S,\operatorname{Diff}_{\mathbb C}^n(E,F))\]

thus defined is an isomorphism.

Proof. By means of the identification (1.2.3.1), we may restrict ourselves to \(n=0\). Setting \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)=G\), we are then reduced to showing that for \(u\in\Gamma(X,\operatorname{pr}_2^*(G))\), the map \(s\longmapsto u_s=i_s^*(u)\) from \(S\) to \(C^\infty(V,G)\) is continuous, and that the linear map
\[\Gamma(X,\operatorname{pr}_2^*(G))\longrightarrow C(S,C^\infty(V,G))\]
thus obtained is an isomorphism. But this follows immediately from the definition of mixed manifolds (1.1).
3.1.5.
Under the hypotheses of (3.1.4), let
\[u\in\operatorname{Diff}^n(\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E),\operatorname{pr}_2^*(F)).\]
Identifying \(u\), by (3.1.4), with a continuous map from \(S\) to \(\operatorname{Diff}^n(E,F)\), one sees that, for \(U\) open in \(S\), the morphism
\[\operatorname{pr}_{1*}(u)(U):\Gamma(X|U,E)\longrightarrow\Gamma(X|U,F)\]
is canonically identified with the linear map
\[C(U,C^\infty(V,E))\longrightarrow C(U,C^\infty(V,F))\]
deduced from
\[\phi u:U\longrightarrow L(C^\infty(V,E),C^\infty(V,F))\qquad(3.1.3).\]
Suppose \(S\) locally compact. Then (3.1.1) the morphism
\[\operatorname{pr}_{1*}(u):\operatorname{pr}_{1*}\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E)\longrightarrow\operatorname{pr}_{1*}\operatorname{pr}_2^*(F)\]
is the morphism of sheaves associated with the morphism of presheaves
\[u_\infty:C'(S,C^\infty(V,E))\longrightarrow C'(S,C^\infty(V,F))\]
defined by \(\phi u\). On the other hand, for \(r\in\mathbb Z\),
\[\phi_ru:S\longrightarrow L(C_2^r(V,E),C_2^{r-n}(V,F))\]
defines a morphism of presheaves
\[u_r:C'(S,C_2^r(V,E))\longrightarrow C'(S,C_2^{r-n}(V,F)).\]
By (3.1.3 (ii)), the \(u_r\) form a projective system whose limit is \(u_\infty\). It will be convenient for us to interpret \(\phi_ru\) as a morphism of trivial Hilbert bundles (3.0.5)
\[S\times C_2^r(V,E)\longrightarrow S\times C_2^{r-n}(V,F),\]
inducing on the sheaves of sections the morphism of sheaves associated with \(u_r\).
3.2. Definition of the index

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.2.1.

Let \(S\) be a locally compact space, \(p:X\longrightarrow S\) a proper mixed manifold, \(E^\bullet\) an elliptic complex of order \(\mathbf n\) of complex vector bundles on \(X\) (1.2.5). Then the complex \(Rp_*(E^\bullet)\) (which, by (2.5), is isomorphic to \(p_*(E^\bullet)\) in the derived category of that of \(\mathcal O_S\otimes\mathbb C\)-Modules) is perfect.

Proof. The question being local on \(S\), we may assume (2.9) that \(X=S\times V\), where \(V\) is a compact manifold, and \(p=\operatorname{pr}_1\). Moreover, we may assume (2.10) that we have, for every \(i\), \(E^i=\operatorname{pr}_2^*(F^i)\), where \(F^i\) is a complex vector bundle of finite rank on \(V\). By (3.1.5), the complex \(p_*(E)\) is associated with the complex of presheaves \((C'(S,C^\infty(V,F^i)),d_\infty^i)\) (where the \(d^i:E^i\longrightarrow E^{i+1}\) are the given differential operators), which we shall denote simply by \(C'(S,C^\infty(V,F^\bullet))\). Set, for each \(i\in\mathbb Z\),
\[m_i=\sum_{j<i}n_j\qquad\text{(on convient que }n_i=0\text{ si }E^i=0).\]

For each \(r\in\mathbb Z\), we have (3.1.5) a complex of presheaves \((C'(S,C_2^{r-m_i}(V,F^i)),d_{r-m_i}^i)\), which we shall denote by \(C'(S,C_2^r(V,F^\bullet))\). The \(C'(S,C_2^r(V,F^\bullet))\) form a projective system, and we have (3.1.5):
\[(3.2.1.1)\qquad C'(S,C^\infty(V,F^\bullet))=\varprojlim_r C'(S,C_2^r(V,F^\bullet)).\]

Finally, we shall denote by \(F_r^\bullet\) the complex of trivial Hilbert bundles on \(S\) defined by \(\phi_{r-m_i}d^i\), and by \(\underline F_r^\bullet\) the complex of the corresponding sheaves of sections, which is none other (3.1.5) than the complex of sheaves associated with \(C'(S,C_2^r(V,F^\bullet))\). That said, to prove the theorem, it suffices to prove the following assertions:
\(a\) For \(r\in\mathbb Z\), the canonical morphism
\[C'(S,C^\infty(V,F^\bullet))\longrightarrow C'(S,C_2^r(V,F^\bullet))\]
is a quasi-isomorphism of complexes of presheaves, i.e. induces isomorphisms on the cohomology presheaves.
\(b\) For \(r\in\mathbb Z\), the complex \(\underline F_r^\bullet\) is perfect.

Proof of \(b\). The ellipticity of \(E^\bullet\) implies ([16] XI Th. 6(1)) that, for every \(s\in S\), the differential of \((F_r^\bullet)_s\) has closed image and that the \(H^i((F_r^\bullet)_s)\) are finite-dimensional vector spaces. In other words, to use the terminology of [11], \(F_r^\bullet\) is a direct quasi-acyclic complex of Hilbert bundles. Consequently, by ([12] 2.3), \(F_r^\bullet\) is locally homotopically equivalent to a bounded complex of vector bundles of finite rank. Since the functor "sheaf of sections" is additive (3.0.5), \(\underline F_r^\bullet\) is locally homotopically equivalent to a bounded complex of locally free sheaves of finite type, hence is perfect.
For the proof of \(a\), we shall need the following intermediate assertion:
\(a'\) For \(p\geq q\), the canonical morphism
\[F_p^\bullet\longrightarrow F_q^\bullet\]
is a quasi-isomorphism.

Proof of \(a'\). Denote by \(M_{pq}^\bullet\) the complex of Hilbert bundles which is the cone of the morphism \(F_p^\bullet\longrightarrow F_q^\bullet\). The ellipticity of \(E\) implies that, for every \(s\in S\), the morphism \((F_p^\bullet)_s\longrightarrow(F_q^\bullet)_s\) is a quasi-isomorphism ([16] XI Cor. 2 of Th. 6). In other words, \(M_{pq}^\bullet\) is a direct acyclic complex. It follows ([11] 2.3) that \(M_{pq}^\bullet\) is locally homotopically trivial, hence that \(\underline M_{pq}^\bullet\), which is none other than the cone of \(\underline F_p^\bullet\longrightarrow\underline F_q^\bullet\), is acyclic, qed.
Proof of \(a\). We must show that, for every relatively compact open subset \(U\) of \(S\), the morphism
\[C(\overline U,C^\infty(V,F^\bullet))\longrightarrow C(\overline U,C_2^r(V,F^\bullet))\]
is a quasi-isomorphism. Replacing \(S\) by \(\overline U\) (and \(E^\bullet\) by the complex induced above \(\overline U\)), we are reduced to showing that
\[C(S,C^\infty(V,F^\bullet))\longrightarrow C(S,C_2^r(V,F^\bullet))\]
is a quasi-isomorphism, \(S\) being assumed compact. By (3.2.1.1) we have
\[C(S,C^\infty(V,F^\bullet))=\varprojlim_r C(S,C_2^r(V,F^\bullet)).\]
For each \(i\), \(C(S,C_2^r(V,F^i))\) is a Banach space (for the topology of uniform convergence), and the transition morphisms
\[C(S,C_2^p(V,F^i))\longrightarrow C(S,C_2^q(V,F^i))\]

are, by virtue of (3.0.4) and (3.1.2), continuous linear maps with dense image. On the other hand, applying to the quasi-isomorphisms \(a'\) \(\underline F_p^\bullet\longrightarrow\underline F_q^\bullet\) the functor \(\Gamma_S\), which is exact since \(\mathcal O_S\) is soft, we see that the transition morphisms
\[C(S,C_2^p(V,F^\bullet))\longrightarrow C(S,C_2^q(V,F^\bullet))\]
are quasi-isomorphisms. Assertion \(a\) then follows from (EGA 0III 13.2.3, "topological" variant). This completes the proof of (3.2.1).
3.2.2.

If \(S\) is a topological space, we shall denote by \(\operatorname{Parf}(S)\) the triangulated category of perfect complexes of \((\mathcal O_S\otimes\mathbb C)\)-Modules, and by \(K'(S)\) the corresponding "Grothendieck group"(1).
Let \(S,X,E^\bullet\) be as in (3.2.1); we call the analytic index of \(E^\bullet\) the element
\[\operatorname{ind.an}(E^\bullet)=\operatorname{Cl}(Rp_*(E^\bullet))\in K'(S).\]

If \(S\) is compact, \(\operatorname{ind.an}(E^\bullet)\) canonically defines an element in the usual group \(K(S)\) of the topologists. Indeed, in a general way, for every topological space \(S\) let us denote by \(\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\) the additive category of locally free \((\mathcal O_S\otimes\mathbb C)\)-Modules of finite type, and by \(K(S)\) the "Grothendieck group" of the triangulated category \(K^b(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))/K^{b,\varnothing}(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\) (the latter is moreover identified with \(K^b(\operatorname{Loclib}(S))\) when \(S\) is paracompact \(II 2.3.2 (a)\), so that in this case (I 6.4) \(K(S)\) is canonically isomorphic to the "Grothendieck group" of the additive category \(\operatorname{Loclib}(S)\)). We have a natural homomorphism
\[K(S)\longrightarrow K'(S).\]
But, by virtue of (II 2.3.2), the latter is an isomorphism for \(S\) compact, whence our assertion. More generally, \(\operatorname{ind.an}(E^\bullet)\) defines an element in the completed group
\[\widehat K(S)=\varprojlim_AK(A),\]
the projective limit being taken over the compact subsets \(A\) of \(S\).
3.2.3.

The editor does not know whether the finiteness theorem (3.2.1) still holds when, instead of assuming \(S\) locally compact, one assumes only that every point of \(S\) admits a fundamental system of paracompact neighborhoods. Moreover, the hypothesis of local compactness on \(S\) does not seem very natural. The space of "universal local parameters" for elliptic complexes of fixed order is indeed an (infinite-dimensional) subspace of a Fréchet space. Let us make this point precise. Let \(V\) be a compact manifold, \(F=(F^i)_{i\in\mathbb Z}\) a sequence of complex vector bundles of finite rank on \(V\), all but finitely many of them zero, and \(\mathbf n=(n_i)\) a sequence of integers \(\geq0\). Denote by \(\operatorname{Comp.diff}^{\mathbf n}(F)\) the space of complexes of differential operators "with base \(F\)" and of order \(\mathbf n\), i.e. the closed subspace of \(\prod_i\operatorname{Diff}^{n_i}(F^i,F^{i+1})\) defined by the equations \(d^{i+1}d^i=0\). (The space \(\prod_i\operatorname{Diff}^{n_i}(F^i,F^{i+1})\) is a Fréchet space.) The subset \(\operatorname{Ell}^{\mathbf n}(F)\) of \(\operatorname{Comp.diff}^{\mathbf n}(F)\) consisting of the elliptic complexes of order \(\mathbf n\) is open; it is this space (which in general is not locally compact) that plays the role of a space of universal local parameters.
3.2.4.

However, even if the generalization of (3.2.1) alluded to above turns out to be impossible, one can still associate with \(E^\bullet\), under the sole hypothesis that the fibration \(p\) be proper and locally trivial, an element of \(K'(S)\) deserving the name of analytic index of \(E^\bullet\). Indeed, suppose (for simplicity(1)) that we are in the "local" situation where \(X=S\times V\) (\(V\), a compact manifold), \(E^\bullet\) being given by a continuous map from \(S\) to \(\operatorname{Ell}^{\mathbf n}(F)\) (3.2.3). The assertions \(a'\) and \(b\) of the proof of (3.2.1) are in fact valid without any hypothesis on \(S\): it is therefore reasonable to set, with the notation of (loc. cit.):
\[\operatorname{ind.an}(E^\bullet)=\operatorname{Cl}(\underline F_r^\bullet)\in K'(S),\]
this element being independent of \(r\).
3.2.5.

Suppose \(S\) paracompact, let \(H\) denote a complex Hilbert space of infinite Hilbert dimension, and \(I(H)\) the subspace of \(L(H,H)\) consisting of the operators "with index". Then the complexes \(F_r^\bullet\) define [12] a canonical element in the group \([S,I(H)]\) of homotopy classes of continuous maps from \(S\) to \(I(H)\), but the editor does not know, for \(S\) non-compact (even in the case where \(S\) is locally compact), what the relations are between the groups \([S,I(S)]\) and \(K'(S)\) (is there a natural arrow from one to the other? are they isomorphic?), hence a fortiori between the two indices thus defined.
For a generalization, under the hypotheses of (3.2.1), of the Atiyah-Singer index theorem, we refer to the recent work of Shih [17], or to a work in preparation by M.F. Atiyah and I.M. Singer.

footnotes.html: To fix ideas (analogous results in the real case).
We omit the index \(\mathbb C\) for brevity.
which generalizes easily to elliptic complexes.
see (I 6.3). For a general review of the functors \(K\), see Exp. IV.
for the general case, use the notion of "weak Hilbert bundle" or continuous field of Hilbert spaces of [12].
